items.question: Yo _____ inglés.
items.translation: (I speak English)
items.explanation: Welcome to Spanish verb conjugation, where every person has its own ending. In English, you use 'I speak' with minimal change across persons. Spanish transforms the verb completely. 'Hablar' (to speak) becomes 'hablo' for 'yo' (I). The -o ending signals first person singular. Think of it as the verb announcing who is speaking. Spanish embeds this information directly into the verb, making the pronoun 'yo' often optional. You could say just 'Hablo inglés' and everyone knows you mean 'I speak'. This richness of verb endings is the heart of Spanish grammar.

items.question: Tú _____ en Madrid.
items.translation: (You live in Madrid)
items.explanation: For 'tú' (informal you), Spanish uses the -es ending. 'Vivir' (to live) becomes 'vives'. English 'you live' stays the same whether formal or informal. Spanish distinguishes: 'tú' for friends, family, and peers, 'usted' for formal situations. The verb ending changes accordingly. 'Vives' tells you immediately this is informal conversation. The -es ending is your marker for second person singular informal. This formality system extends throughout Spanish - choosing between tú and usted affects not just pronouns but verb forms, possessives, and commands.

items.question: Ella _____ café.
items.translation: (She drinks coffee)
items.explanation: Third person singular uses the -e ending. 'Beber' (to drink) becomes 'bebe' for él/ella/usted. English uses 'drinks' with added -s. Spanish uses 'bebe' - a completely different ending. This -e ending serves triple duty: he, she, and formal you. Context tells you which. 'Ella bebe café' is clear because of 'ella', but 'Bebe café' alone could mean he, she, or formal you. The beauty is in the flexibility - the verb form unites these three subjects while remaining distinct from other persons.

items.question: Nosotros _____ español.
items.translation: (We study Spanish)
items.explanation: First person plural takes the -amos ending. 'Estudiar' (to study) becomes 'estudiamos'. English 'we study' uses the base form. Spanish 'estudiamos' is unmistakable - only 'nosotros' uses this ending. The -amos pattern appears across all regular -ar verbs, making it predictable once you learn the pattern. This consistency is helpful - master one conjugation pattern and you can apply it to hundreds of verbs. 'Nosotros' is often dropped in conversation because 'estudiamos' alone identifies the subject perfectly.

items.question: Ellos _____ en casa.
items.translation: (They work at home)
items.explanation: Third person plural uses the -an ending. 'Trabajar' (to work) becomes 'trabajan' for ellos/ellas/ustedes. English 'they work' is simple. Spanish 'trabajan' immediately signals multiple people. The -an ending also covers formal plural (ustedes). Spanish doesn't distinguish informal vs formal in plural like it does in singular. Whether speaking to friends or bosses, plural 'you' is always 'ustedes' with -an verbs. This simplification in plural is a relief after navigating tú vs usted in singular.

items.question: Yo _____ la puerta.
items.translation: (I open the door)
items.explanation: First person of 'abrir' (to open) is 'abro'. This -ir verb follows a slightly different pattern than -ar verbs, but the principle is the same. The -o ending still marks first person. Spanish has three verb families: -ar, -er, and -ir. Each has its own ending patterns, though -er and -ir are quite similar. 'Abrir' belongs to the -ir family. Once you learn the three patterns, you can conjugate thousands of Spanish verbs. The system is regular and logical, rewarding careful study with predictable results.

items.question: Tú _____ el libro.
items.translation: (You read the book)
items.explanation: For 'leer' (to read), an -er verb, 'tú' takes -es. This is the same ending as -ir verbs for second person. The pattern holds across verb families. English 'you read' doesn't change from 'I read'. Spanish gives each person its signature. 'Lees' is unmistakably 'tú' form. This consistency across similar verb families helps learning - once you know -ar verbs well, -er and -ir verbs follow comparable patterns with slight variations.

items.question: Nosotros _____ la verdad.
items.translation: (We write the truth)
items.explanation: First person plural of 'escribir' (to write) uses -imos. This is the -ir equivalent of -amos for -ar verbs. The pattern shifts slightly between verb families, but remains regular within each family. 'Escribimos' clearly signals 'we' are writing. Spanish verb endings carry so much information that pronouns become optional. The verb alone tells you who, when, and what. This efficiency is elegant - one word can express what English needs several words to convey.

items.question: Ellas _____ mucho.
items.translation: (They eat a lot)
items.explanation: Third person plural of 'comer' (to eat) is 'comen'. The -en ending works for both -er and -ir verbs in this person. Spanish unifies these endings in third person plural, making it easier to remember. Whether formal or informal, plural subjects use the same ending. 'Ellas comen', 'ellos comen', 'ustedes comen' - all use 'comen'. This consistency within persons helps you build confidence as you learn.

items.question: Usted _____ razón.
items.translation: (You are right)
items.explanation: Formal 'you' (usted) takes the same verb form as él/ella. 'Tener' (to have) becomes 'tiene'. This verb is irregular, but the person-ending relationship holds. Even irregular verbs follow the pattern of distinct endings for each person. 'Tiene' works for he/she/formal you. The formality is shown through 'usted' and the third person verb form. This is one of Spanish's charming quirks - treating formal 'you' as 'he/she' grammatically, showing linguistic respect.

items.question: Yo _____ sed.
items.translation: (I am thirsty)
items.explanation: 'Tener' is irregular, but first person still gets its distinctive -go ending. Many common Spanish verbs are irregular, yet they maintain the person-marking system. 'Tengo' is recognizably first person despite the irregularity. Spanish expressions for physical states often use 'tener' (to have) where English uses 'to be'. 'Tengo sed' literally means 'I have thirst'. This difference in conceptualization is fascinating - Spanish views these states as things you possess, not states of being.

items.question: Ellos _____ a la tienda.
items.translation: (They go to the store)
items.explanation: 'Ir' (to go) is highly irregular, but 'van' still clearly marks third person plural. Even the most irregular verbs maintain distinct forms for each person. 'Van' couldn't be confused with other persons - it's uniquely theirs. Spanish tolerates irregularity in common verbs because they're used so frequently that the forms become automatic. The trade-off is worth it - high-frequency verbs earn the right to be exceptional.

items.question: Tú _____ cansado.
items.translation: (You are tired)
items.explanation: 'Estar' (to be) is irregular, giving 'estás' for 'tú'. Spanish has two verbs for 'to be': ser and estar. 'Estar' describes temporary states like being tired. The 'tú' form gets -ás, unique to this verb. Even in irregularity, Spanish maintains its person-marking devotion. Each person gets its recognizable form. This dual 'to be' system allows Spanish to distinguish between inherent qualities (ser) and temporary states (estar) - a distinction English makes through context.

items.question: Nosotros _____ al parque.
items.translation: (We go to the park)
items.explanation: First person plural of irregular 'ir' is 'vamos'. Despite being one of Spanish's most irregular verbs, 'vamos' is unmistakably 'we'. The -amos ending echoes regular -ar verbs, even in this irregular context. 'Vamos' is also used for 'let's go' - a gentle invitation. One word carries both meanings, with context clarifying which is intended. This economy of expression is characteristic of Spanish.

items.question: Yo _____ español.
items.translation: (I am Spanish)
items.explanation: 'Ser' (to be) gives 'soy' for 'yo', describing permanent characteristics or identity. This is Spanish's other 'to be' verb, used for inherent qualities. 'Soy español' means being Spanish is your identity. The distinction between ser and estar is subtle but important. Ser for what you are, estar for how you are. 'Soy feliz' (I'm a happy person by nature) vs 'estoy feliz' (I'm happy right now). This linguistic precision allows for nuanced expression of states and being.

items.question: _____ libro es interesante.
items.translation: (The book is interesting)
items.explanation: Spanish articles have gender. 'Libro' (book) is masculine, so 'el' (the). English 'the' works for everything. Spanish assigns gender to all nouns - masculine or feminine. This gender affects articles, adjectives, and sometimes verbs. 'El' is your masculine definite article, used when referring to a specific masculine noun. Learning noun genders is essential in Spanish. There are patterns - words ending in -o tend masculine, -a tend feminine - but many exceptions exist. Mastering gender takes time and exposure.

items.question: _____ casa es grande.
items.translation: (The house is big)
items.explanation: 'Casa' (house) is feminine, so 'la' (the). Spanish assigns feminine gender to 'house', different from some other languages. Memorizing which nouns are which gender is part of learning Spanish. 'La' is your feminine definite article. It signals you're talking about a specific feminine noun. Gender agreement extends through the sentence - 'la casa grande' (the big house) shows feminine gender on article and adjective. This consistency helps once you internalize the system.

items.question: Tengo _____ perro.
items.translation: (I have a dog)
items.explanation: 'Perro' (dog) is masculine, so 'un' (a/an). Spanish indefinite articles also have gender. English 'a' or 'an' becomes 'un' (masculine) or 'una' (feminine). When introducing something new or non-specific, use indefinite articles. 'Un perro' means some dog, any dog, not a particular one you've mentioned before. The progression from indefinite to definite is natural in Spanish - introduce with un/una, refer back with el/la.

items.question: Es _____ profesora.
items.translation: (She is a teacher)
items.explanation: 'Profesora' (teacher, female) is feminine, so 'una'. Spanish differentiates teacher genders: profesor (male), profesora (female). English 'teacher' is gender-neutral. Spanish marks gender on professions, with -o/-a endings typically. When stating someone's profession with ser, Spanish often uses indefinite articles where English might omit them. 'Es una profesora' feels more natural than English 'She is a teacher'. The article softens the statement, making it descriptive rather than defining.

items.question: _____ gatos son bonitos.
items.translation: (The cats are pretty)
items.explanation: Plural masculine uses 'los'. 'Gatos' (cats) plural of 'gato' (masculine). Spanish pluralizes articles along with nouns. 'El' becomes 'los', 'la' becomes 'las'. Gender carries into plural - even mixed groups default to masculine plural. Ten female cats and one male cat would be 'los gatos'. This masculine default for mixed groups is grammatical convention, not social commentary. It simplifies the system by having one fallback for ambiguous situations.

items.question: _____ amigas hablan mucho.
items.translation: (The friends talk a lot)
items.explanation: Plural feminine uses 'las'. 'Amigas' (friends, female) is feminine plural. A group of all females takes 'las'. The -as ending signals feminine plural. Spanish maintains gender distinction in plurals. 'Las amigas' specifies all female friends. If there were any males in the group, it would shift to 'los amigos'. This gender marking provides precision about group composition.

items.question: Quiero _____ libros.
items.translation: (I want some books)
items.explanation: Plural indefinite masculine is 'unos'. 'Libros' (books) plural masculine. English 'some books' or just 'books'. Spanish can use 'unos/unas' for 'some' or omit for general plurals. 'Quiero libros' (I want books generally) vs 'Quiero unos libros' (I want some specific books). The article adds specificity even in indefinite plural. Spanish offers more granular distinctions than English in article use.

items.question: Me gustan _____ flores.
items.translation: (I like flowers)
items.explanation: When talking about things in general, Spanish uses definite articles where English doesn't. 'Me gustan las flores' means 'I like flowers' (flowers in general). English omits 'the', Spanish includes 'las'. This is a key difference - Spanish treats general categories as definite. 'Los perros son leales' (dogs are loyal) uses 'los' for the general concept. Once you internalize this pattern, it becomes natural.

items.question: _____ agua es fría.
items.translation: (The water is cold)
items.explanation: Feminine nouns starting with stressed 'a' take 'el' to ease pronunciation. 'Agua' is feminine but uses 'el'. This prevents awkward 'la agua' sound. The noun remains feminine - 'el agua fría' (cold water) uses feminine adjective. This exception proves Spanish prioritizes smooth speech. Other examples: 'el alma' (the soul), 'el águila' (the eagle). Remember: 'el' with feminine nouns starting with stressed 'a' or 'ha', but adjectives stay feminine.

items.question: Voy _____ colegio.
items.translation: (I go to school)
items.explanation: 'A' + 'el' contracts to 'al'. Spanish contracts preposition + article in two cases. 'A el' becomes 'al', 'de el' becomes 'del'. 'Colegio' is masculine, so 'voy al colegio'. This contraction is mandatory, not optional. English 'to the' stays separate. Spanish merges them for smoother flow. Similarly, 'libro del profesor' (the teacher's book) uses 'del' from 'de el'. These contractions become automatic quickly.

items.question: _____ mujeres son inteligentes.
items.translation: (Women are intelligent)
items.explanation: General statements about groups use definite articles. 'Las mujeres' for women as a category. English 'women are intelligent' omits articles. Spanish includes them for general categories. This pattern extends to all general statements: 'los hombres' (men), 'los niños' (children). The definite article paradoxically makes the statement more general, not more specific. Think of it as referring to the entire category as a known entity.

items.question: No me gusta _____ café.
items.translation: (I don't like coffee)
items.explanation: Likes and dislikes use definite articles for general things. 'Me gusta el café' means coffee in general. English 'I like coffee' drops the article. Spanish perspective: 'Coffee pleases me', treating it as a specific concept. This construction with gustar treats the thing liked as the subject - 'el café me gusta' (coffee pleases me). The definite article is required even when speaking generally. It's a fixed pattern with gustar.

items.question: Hablo _____ español.
items.translation: (I speak Spanish)
items.explanation: Languages used with verbs like hablar (speak), aprender (learn), estudiar (study) take no article. 'Hablo español', not 'hablo el español'. This is an exception to Spanish's article-loving nature. When language is the direct object of these verbs, omit the article. But 'El español es difícil' (Spanish is difficult) needs 'el' because Spanish is the subject. Context determines article use with languages - as objects of these specific verbs, no article needed.

items.question: _____ señor García es amable.
items.translation: (Mr. García is kind)
items.explanation: Titles before names take definite articles when talking about the person. 'El señor García' (Mr. García). English omits articles with titles. Spanish includes them when referring to titled people. But direct address drops it: 'Buenos días, señor García' (Good morning, Mr. García). This subtle distinction shows respect in reference while maintaining directness in address. The pattern holds for all titles: la señora, el doctor, la profesora.

items.question: Me duele _____ cabeza.
items.translation: (My head hurts)
items.explanation: Body parts use definite articles where English uses possessives. 'Me duele la cabeza' not 'mi cabeza'. Spanish perspective: the context (me duele) clarifies whose head. Using 'la' instead of 'mi' is standard with body parts and clothing. 'Me lavo las manos' (I wash my hands), 'Me pongo los zapatos' (I put on my shoes). The definite article suffices because the reflexive or indirect object pronoun indicates possession. This pattern is consistent and becomes natural with practice.

items.question: Ayer yo _____ al cine.
items.translation: (Yesterday I went to the cinema)
items.explanation: The preterite expresses completed past actions. 'Fui' is the irregular preterite of 'ir' (to go). English 'went' is similarly irregular. Spanish preterite marks actions with clear beginning and end. 'Ayer fui' indicates a complete action yesterday. This differs from imperfect, which describes ongoing or habitual past actions. Learning when to use preterite versus imperfect is a key Spanish skill. Start with this: preterite for completed, one-time actions with definable endpoints.

items.question: Ella _____ la carta.
items.translation: (She wrote the letter)
items.explanation: Regular preterite for 'escribir' adds specific endings. 'Escribió' marks completed past action. The letter is finished. English past simple 'wrote' is conceptually similar. Preterite endings for -ir verbs: -í, -iste, -ió, -imos, -isteis, -ieron. These endings work for hundreds of regular verbs. The 'ió' ending is distinctly third person singular preterite. Once you learn the pattern, you can conjugate new verbs immediately.

items.question: Nosotros _____ español.
items.translation: (We studied Spanish)
items.explanation: Interestingly, 'estudiamos' is identical in present and preterite for 'nosotros'. Context disambiguates. 'Ayer estudiamos' (yesterday we studied) vs 'Hoy estudiamos' (today we study). This overlap occurs only for -ar and -ir verbs in first person plural. Spanish relies on context - time markers like 'ayer' (yesterday) or 'siempre' (always) clarify tense. Native speakers navigate this ambiguity effortlessly. You will too, with practice.

items.question: Tú _____ la película.
items.translation: (You watched the movie)
items.explanation: Irregular 'ver' (to see) gives 'viste' in preterite. Many common verbs have irregular preterite forms. 'Viste' specifically marks second person singular completed action. The irregularity groups into patterns - stem-changing preterites, -u- stem verbs, etc. Learning these groups helps. 'Ver' belongs to short-stem irregulars (vi, viste, vio). Though irregular, each form is distinct for its person. The system maintains its person-marking devotion even in irregularity.

items.question: Ellos _____ a las ocho.
items.translation: (They arrived at eight)
items.explanation: Regular -ar preterite third person plural: -aron. 'Llegaron' indicates completed arrival. The action happened at a specific time (eight o'clock). Preterite loves specific time markers. 'A las ocho' (at eight) signals preterite territory. The -aron ending clearly marks third person plural past. Regular preterite endings are predictable: -é, -aste, -ó, -amos, -asteis, -aron for -ar verbs. Master one pattern, apply to hundreds of verbs.

items.question: Yo _____ un email.
items.translation: (I sent an email)
items.explanation: First person preterite of regular -ar verb 'mandar': 'mandé'. The accent on -é distinguishes it from present 'mande' (command form). Spanish uses accents to preserve pronunciation and meaning. The 'é' ending is uniquely first person preterite for -ar verbs. 'Mandé' marks a completed sending action. The email is sent, action finished. English 'sent' conveys similar completion. Both languages mark completion, Spanish through specific endings.

items.question: Ella _____ en Madrid.
items.translation: (She was born in Madrid)
items.explanation: 'Nacer' (to be born) always uses preterite - birth is a completed, one-time event. 'Nació' expresses this definitively finished action. Some verbs naturally favor preterite because of their inherent completeness. Birth, death, beginnings, endings - these gravitate toward preterite. The verb itself guides tense choice. 'Nació en Madrid' can only be preterite - the action is permanently complete and in the past.

items.question: Nosotros _____ el museo.
items.translation: (We visited the museum)
items.explanation: Another present/preterite overlap for 'nosotros'. 'Visitamos' serves both tenses. Context makes meaning clear. If discussing yesterday, it's preterite. If describing habits, present. Time expressions are your guide. 'El sábado pasado visitamos' (last Saturday we visited) is clearly preterite. 'Los sábados visitamos' (on Saturdays we visit) is present habit. Spanish speakers use context naturally. You will too.

items.question: Tú _____ en el parque.
items.translation: (You ran in the park)
items.explanation: Regular -er preterite second person: 'corriste'. The -iste ending marks this person and tense combination. English 'ran' is irregular. Spanish 'corriste' follows the pattern. The completed run is expressed through preterite. Whether once around the park or a marathon, completed running uses preterite. The -er verb endings for preterite mirror -ir verbs: -í, -iste, -ió, -imos, -isteis, -ieron. This parallel helps learning.

items.question: Yo _____ tarde.
items.translation: (I woke up late)
items.explanation: Reflexive verb preterite: 'despertarse' becomes 'me desperté'. The reflexive pronoun 'me' stays, verb conjugates normally. Preterite marks the completed waking. The action happened and finished. Reflexive verbs follow all the same tense rules, just keeping their pronouns. 'Me desperté tarde' - woke late, complete action, specific past moment. Preterite is appropriate because waking is a defined action with clear completion.

items.question: Ellos _____ la verdad.
items.translation: (They found out the truth)
items.explanation: 'Saber' (to know) in preterite means 'found out'. This verb changes meaning in preterite. 'Sabían' (imperfect) means they knew (ongoing state). 'Supieron' means they found out (moment of learning). Several Spanish verbs shift meaning between preterite and imperfect. This isn't randomness - it reflects how we conceptualize these verbs. Finding out is a moment (preterite). Knowing is a state (imperfect). Understanding these meaning shifts deepens your Spanish.

items.question: Ayer yo _____ mucho.
items.translation: (Yesterday I worked a lot)
items.explanation: Yesterday signals completed action, so preterite 'trabajé'. Time markers guide tense choice. 'Ayer' (yesterday), 'anoche' (last night), 'el año pasado' (last year) - these call for preterite. The action occurred in a completed time frame. 'Trabajé mucho' describes intensive work that's now finished. The -é ending marks first person completion. These endings become automatic with practice.

items.question: Ella no _____ venir.
items.translation: (She couldn't come)
items.explanation: 'Poder' (can/to be able) in preterite often means 'managed to' or 'succeeded in'. 'No pudo' means she tried but couldn't. This is another meaning-shifting verb. 'No podía' (imperfect) means she wasn't able to (general inability). 'No pudo' means she failed to (specific attempt). The tense choice changes the story. Preterite focuses on the result of trying. Imperfect describes the state of inability.

items.question: Tú _____ un regalo.
items.translation: (You received a gift)
items.explanation: Regular -ir preterite: 'recibiste'. Receiving a gift is a completed action - perfect for preterite. The moment you received it is in the past. The -iste ending marks second person preterite for -er/-ir verbs. Regular patterns make Spanish learnable. Even new verbs follow these rules. 'Recibiste' clearly communicates completed past action in second person. Context might add when, but the verb already tells who and that it's finished.

items.question: Nosotros _____ felices.
items.translation: (We were happy)
items.explanation: 'Ser' preterite 'fuimos' describes temporary state or event. The choice between 'fuimos' (preterite) and 'éramos' (imperfect) depends on duration. 'Fuimos felices' suggests happiness during a specific event or period. 'Éramos felices' implies ongoing childhood happiness. The distinction is subtle but meaningful. Preterite 'fuimos' frames happiness as belonging to a completed time frame. This is advanced Spanish nuance, coming with exposure and practice.

items.question: Yo _____ de México.
items.translation: (I am from Mexico)
items.explanation: 'Ser' expresses origin and identity. 'Soy de México' states where you're from - a permanent fact. English uses one 'to be', Spanish uses two. 'Ser' for inherent characteristics, identity, origin, time, material, possession, relationships. Think of ser as the 'essence' verb. What something fundamentally is. This distinction between ser and estar allows Spanish to express nuance English achieves through context or extra words.

items.question: Ella _____ profesora.
items.translation: (She is a teacher)
items.explanation: Professions use 'ser'. Being a teacher is an identity, so 'es profesora'. This is what she is, not a temporary condition. 'Ser' connects subject to defining noun. 'Es profesora' tells you her profession, a relatively stable characteristic. This is her role, her identity in work. 'Ser' claims this as part of who she is. The verb choice reflects how Spanish conceptualizes professions - as identities, not temporary states.

items.question: El libro _____ de papel.
items.translation: (The book is made of paper)
items.explanation: Material composition uses 'ser'. 'Es de papel' tells what it's made of. This is inherent to the book - it can't be made of something else and still be this book. 'Ser de' construction indicates material. Books are fundamentally paper (or digital now, but traditionally). This physical composition is a defining characteristic. 'Ser' makes sense - it's stating what the thing essentially is, material-wise.

items.question: Nosotros _____ estudiantes.
items.translation: (We are students)
items.explanation: Identity as students uses 'ser'. 'Somos estudiantes' states your role/identity. Being a student is how you define yourself in this context. It's not temporary like being tired. You are students - this is your current identity. 'Ser' connects you to this defining noun. The verb choice shows Spanish treats roles and professions as part of identity, using the essence verb.

items.question: La fiesta _____ mañana.
items.translation: (The party is tomorrow)
items.explanation: Time and date use 'ser'. 'La fiesta es mañana' states when the party occurs. Events in time use 'ser'. Also hours: 'Son las tres' (It's three o'clock). Time is treated as an inherent property of events. The party exists at this time essentially. This is a fixed feature of the event. 'Ser' makes sense - the timing defines the event. It is tomorrow, not later.

items.question: Yo _____ en casa.
items.translation: (I am at home)
items.explanation: 'Estar' expresses location. 'Estoy en casa' tells where you are right now. Location is temporary - you won't always be home. 'Estar' for physical location is a core rule. Even if you're home every day, each time is a temporary state. Location can change. 'Estar' reflects this impermanence. You exist in this place now. This is 'estar' territory - position, not essence.

items.question: Ella _____ feliz hoy.
items.translation: (She is happy today)
items.explanation: Emotions and temporary states use 'estar'. 'Está feliz' means she's happy right now. This could change. 'Ser feliz' would mean being a happy person by nature. The verb choice changes meaning. 'Estar' emphasizes the current, changeable nature of happiness. She is experiencing happiness. This is her current emotional state. 'Estar' captures the temporary quality of this feeling.

items.question: El café _____ caliente.
items.translation: (The coffee is hot)
items.explanation: Temperature uses 'estar' - it's a current, temporary condition. 'Está caliente' means hot right now. Coffee cools. This state is transient. 'Estar' reflects changeable physical conditions. Temperature, wetness, cleanliness - these use 'estar'. They describe current state, not inherent nature. The coffee isn't fundamentally hot. It is currently hot. This temporary state calls for 'estar'.

items.question: La puerta _____ abierta.
items.translation: (The door is open)
items.explanation: Position/state uses 'estar'. 'Está abierta' means currently open. Doors open and close - this is temporary. 'Estar' describes the door's current state. Open or closed is not the door's essence. It's a changeable condition. 'Estar' captures this. Past participles describing state use 'estar': abierta, cerrada, rota. These are results of actions, temporary conditions.

items.question: Ellos _____ inteligentes.
items.translation: (They are intelligent)
items.explanation: Inherent characteristics use 'ser'. 'Son inteligentes' describes their nature. Intelligence is a defining trait. This isn't temporary - it's who they are. 'Ser' connects them to this quality as part of their essence. Personality traits, physical descriptions, inherent qualities all use 'ser'. These define the person. They are the person's characteristics, not temporary states.

items.question: El examen _____ difícil.
items.translation: (The exam is difficult)
items.explanation: Inherent qualities of things use 'ser'. The exam is fundamentally difficult - that's its nature. 'Es difícil' describes what the exam is like essentially. This isn't about current state. The exam's difficulty level is a defining characteristic. 'Ser' works for objective qualities. If you meant it seems difficult to you personally, 'está difícil' could work, but normally 'es' for objective difficulty.

items.question: Nosotros _____ cansados.
items.translation: (We are tired)
items.explanation: Physical/mental states use 'estar'. 'Estamos cansados' means currently tired. Tiredness is temporary. Rest will change it. 'Estar' captures this transient quality. Physical sensations and conditions use 'estar': tired, sick, hungry. These are passing states. You experience them now. They will change. 'Estar' reflects this impermanence.

items.question: Las manzanas _____ verdes.
items.translation: (The apples are green)
items.explanation: Color typically uses 'ser' for inherent color. 'Son verdes' means green apples - that's their type. Green is their defining characteristic. However, 'están verdes' could mean unripe. This is a meaning distinction. 'Ser verde' = green color. 'Estar verde' = unripe state. The verb choice changes what you're saying about the apples. Usually color uses 'ser' as a defining feature.

items.question: La película _____ aburrida.
items.translation: (The movie is boring)
items.explanation: Qualities of things use 'ser'. The movie is inherently boring - that's its quality. 'Es aburrida' describes what the movie is like as a film. This is objective assessment of the movie itself. If you meant you find it boring (subjective experience), 'está aburrida' could work in some dialects. But standard usage: inherent qualities use 'ser'. The movie's boringness is its characteristic.

items.question: Tú _____ muy guapo hoy.
items.translation: (You look very handsome today)
items.explanation: 'Today' signals temporary state, so 'estar'. 'Estás guapo' means you look handsome today (maybe dressed up). 'Eres guapo' means you're a handsome person generally. The addition of 'hoy' (today) points toward 'estar'. You're looking particularly good today - a temporary enhanced state. This subtle distinction allows Spanish to express nuance. The same adjective with different verbs conveys different meanings.

items.question: Mañana yo _____ al médico.
items.translation: (Tomorrow I will go to the doctor)
items.explanation: Simple future attaches endings to the infinitive. 'Ir' becomes 'iré' for first person. English 'will go' uses auxiliary. Spanish future is one word with distinctive endings: -é, -ás, -á, -emos, -éis, -án. These endings work for all verbs, regular and irregular. The pattern is beautifully consistent. Once you learn these six endings, you can form future tense for any verb. The accents preserve pronunciation and distinguish these endings from other tenses.

items.question: Ellos _____ mañana.
items.translation: (They will arrive tomorrow)
items.explanation: Third person plural future: infinitive + -án. 'Llegar' + án = 'llegarán'. The -án ending clearly marks future third person plural. All verbs follow this pattern. The consistency is helpful - no need to learn separate future forms. Just add endings to infinitive. 'Mañana' (tomorrow) confirms future time frame. The verb agrees with this. Spanish future tense is refreshingly regular compared to past tenses.

items.question: Nosotros _____ español.
items.translation: (We will study Spanish)
items.explanation: First person plural: infinitive + -emos. 'Estudiar' + emos = 'estudiaremos'. The -emos ending is unique to future first person plural. Spanish future endings come from the Latin verb 'haber' (to have), explaining their similarity to present tense of haber. This historical connection makes the system logical. The future literally evolved from 'I have to study' becoming 'I will study'.

items.question: Tú _____ el libro.
items.translation: (You will read the book)
items.explanation: Second person future: infinitive + -ás. 'Leer' + ás = 'leerás'. The accent on -á distinguishes this from other tenses. Future tense is simple to form - far easier than preterite or imperfect. The trade-off is that spoken Spanish often uses 'ir a + infinitive' (going to) for future. But formal writing loves simple future. Both forms are correct. 'Leerás' sounds more literary than 'vas a leer'.

items.question: Ella _____ médica.
items.translation: (She will be a doctor)
items.explanation: 'Ser' has irregular future stem 'ser-', giving 'será'. Some common verbs shorten their stem in future. 'Ser' drops the 'e' from 'seer'. 'Será médica' expresses future profession. The irregularity affects only the stem. Endings remain standard. Irregular future stems cluster into patterns: drop vowels (habr-, cabr-, sabr-), replace with 'd' (pondr-, tendr-, saldr-, vendr-). Learning these patterns helps. 'Será' is commonly used - 'will be' is frequent in all languages.

items.question: Yo _____ la carta.
items.translation: (I will write the letter)
items.explanation: Regular future: 'escribir' + é = 'escribiré'. The pattern is consistent and predictable. First person future always ends in -é with accent. This accent is grammatically significant - it marks the stressed syllable and distinguishes future from preterite. 'Escribiré' is one clear word expressing future action. The simplicity of formation makes Spanish future accessible to learners. Add endings, done.

items.question: ¿_____ ayudarme?
items.translation: (Will you help me?)
items.explanation: 'Poder' has irregular stem 'podr-'. Future: 'podrás'. Asking if someone will be able to help uses future tense naturally. The 'd' insertion pattern appears in several verbs: poder → podr-, poner → pondr-, tener → tendr-, venir → vendr-, salir → saldr-. These verbs drop the vowel from their stem and insert 'd'. The pattern makes them learnable as a group. Once you spot the pattern, you can predict the forms.

items.question: Ellos _____ la verdad.
items.translation: (They will know the truth)
items.explanation: 'Saber' future stem drops 'e': 'sabr-', giving 'sabrán'. The pattern of dropping stem vowels creates these shortened forms. 'Sabrán' sounds smoother than 'saberán' would. Spanish phonetics influence grammar. The future forms evolved toward ease of pronunciation. These irregular stems become automatic with use. 'Sabrán' expresses future knowledge or finding out. The certainty of future tense suggests inevitability.

items.question: Nosotros _____ tarde.
items.translation: (We will arrive late)
items.explanation: Regular future: 'llegar' + emos = 'llegaremos'. The formation is straightforward. First person plural future is recognizable by -emos ending. This parallels the -amos present ending but with different vowel. The accent falls naturally on -e- without needing written accent. 'Llegaremos tarde' is a simple statement of future lateness. The future tense carries a sense of certainty about this lateness.

items.question: ¿_____ tiempo?
items.translation: (Will you have time?)
items.explanation: 'Tener' future stem is 'tendr-', giving 'tendrás'. This is a key irregular verb to learn. The 'd' insertion pattern repeats across related verbs. 'Tendrás tiempo' asks about future availability. The future tense frames this as genuine future question. Spanish uses future tense more formally than English, which often uses present for near future. 'Tendrás' sounds more proper than 'tienes' for future events.

items.question: Yo _____ el mes próximo.
items.translation: (I will leave next month)
items.explanation: 'Salir' future: 'saldr-' + é = 'saldré'. Another 'd' insertion irregular. The pattern is productive - if you know one, you can guess others. 'Saldré' expresses definite future departure. 'El mes próximo' (next month) confirms distant future. Future tense feels appropriate for planned events. The certainty in the verb matches planned actions. 'Saldré' conveys commitment to the departure.

items.question: Mañana _____ mucho trabajo.
items.translation: (Tomorrow there will be a lot of work)
items.explanation: 'Haber' future stem is 'habr-', third person 'habrá'. The impersonal 'hay' (there is/are) becomes 'habrá' (there will be). This is commonly used for future existence statements. 'Habrá trabajo' means work will exist. The shortened stem 'habr-' follows the vowel-dropping pattern. 'Habrá' is useful for predictions and future conditions. It's one of the most common future forms in speech.

items.question: Tú _____ a la fiesta.
items.translation: (You will come to the party)
items.explanation: 'Venir' (to come) future: 'vendr-' + ás = 'vendrás'. The 'd' pattern continues. This is one of the core irregular futures to master. 'Vendrás' expresses expected future arrival. Invitations and plans naturally use future tense. The verb choice conveys expectation or invitation. 'Vendrás' can be statement or question with intonation. Spanish is flexible with word order for questions.

items.question: Ella _____ a casa temprano.
items.translation: (She will return home early)
items.explanation: 'Volver' is regular in future: 'volver' + á = 'volverá'. Not all common verbs are irregular. The stem-changing verbs (o→ue like volver) are regular in future. The stem change happens only in present tense. Future uses the full infinitive. This is a relief - future tense has fewer irregularities than present. 'Volverá temprano' states planned early return. The future tense suits scheduled events.

items.question: Nosotros _____ la película.
items.translation: (We will watch the movie)
items.explanation: 'Ver' future: 'ver' + emos = 'veremos'. Despite being irregular in other tenses, ver has regular future. The -emos ending marks first person plural. 'Veremos' expresses planned viewing. This is straightforward future formation. The verb remains full infinitive with endings added. 'Veremos la película' is a common construction for future plans. The future tense adds certainty to the plan.

items.question: _____ la puerta. (tú)
items.translation: (Close the door)
items.explanation: Informal 'tú' commands use third person present form. 'Cerrar' becomes 'cierra'. This is one of Spanish's elegant shortcuts. Take the él/ella form, use it as command. The simplicity helps memory. 'Cierra la puerta' is a direct, informal command. The tú command sounds friendly and direct. Use it with people you know well. The form implies familiarity and comfort in the relationship.

items.question: _____ aquí. (usted)
items.translation: (Come here - formal)
items.explanation: Formal 'usted' commands use present subjunctive. 'Venir' subjunctive gives 'venga'. The formality shows in the verb choice. Subjunctive for commands adds politeness and distance. 'Venga aquí' is respectfully directive. Use with strangers, elders, or professional contexts. The subjunctive connection means learning subjunctive helps with formal commands. They're the same forms.

items.question: _____ la verdad. (tú)
items.translation: (Tell the truth)
items.explanation: 'Decir' has irregular tú command: 'di'. Some common verbs have special short commands for tú. These irregular commands are worth memorizing: di (decir), haz (hacer), ven (venir), pon (poner), sal (salir), ten (tener), ve (ir). These short forms feel natural and are deeply embedded in Spanish. 'Di la verdad' is direct and informal. The brevity adds urgency.

items.question: _____ los platos. (vosotros)
items.translation: (Wash the dishes - you all)
items.explanation: Vosotros commands: infinitive minus -r plus -d. 'Lavar' becomes 'lavad'. This is the simplest command formation. Spain's informal plural has this straightforward rule. Replace final -r with -d. 'Lavad los platos' addresses a group informally. Outside Spain, this form is rarely used. Latin America uses ustedes for all plural, formal or informal. But in Spain, vosotros is everyday speech among friends.

items.question: _____ el libro. (usted)
items.translation: (Read the book - formal)
items.explanation: Formal command uses subjunctive. 'Leer' subjunctive: 'lea'. The -a ending marks formal command for -er/-ir verbs. Regular pattern: -ar verbs use -e, -er/-ir verbs use -a in subjunctive/commands. 'Lea el libro' is politely directive. The formality respects the listener. Professional or formal settings call for usted commands. They maintain appropriate social distance while being directive.

items.question: No _____ tarde. (tú)
items.translation: (Don't arrive late)
items.explanation: Negative commands always use subjunctive for all persons. 'No llegues' uses present subjunctive. Positive tú commands differ from negative. Positive: 'llega'. Negative: 'no llegues'. This asymmetry is important. All negative commands are subjunctive. The 'no' triggers subjunctive mood. 'No llegues tarde' is firm but familiar. Negative commands feel more serious, the subjunctive adds this gravity.

items.question: _____ más ejercicio. (usted)
items.translation: (Do more exercise - formal)
items.explanation: 'Hacer' formal command: 'haga'. Irregular subjunctive gives irregular command. The stem changes to 'hag-'. Common verbs often have irregularities you need to memorize. 'Haga ejercicio' is polite advice or instruction. Doctor's advice, formal recommendations use usted commands. The formality shows professional relationship. 'Haga' is respectfully directive without being harsh.

items.question: _____ buenos. (vosotros)
items.translation: (Be good - you all)
items.explanation: 'Ser' has irregular vosotros command: 'sed'. Most irregular tú commands have regular vosotros forms, but 'ser' is special. 'Sed buenos' is parental or teacher language in Spain. Addressing a group of children to behave. The vosotros command feels warm and familiar. It's for your people, your group. The irregularity of 'sed' is historical, preserved because the verb is so common.

items.question: No _____ eso. (usted)
items.translation: (Don't do that - formal)
items.explanation: Negative formal command uses subjunctive. 'No haga eso' is polite prohibition. The formality softens the prohibition. You're telling someone not to do something, but respectfully. Usted commands maintain politeness even in prohibition. 'No haga' is firm but courteous. Professional settings, service interactions use these forms. They balance directiveness with respect.

items.question: _____ conmigo. (tú)
items.translation: (Come with me)
items.explanation: 'Venir' irregular tú command: 'ven'. Short and sweet. This is one of the special short tú commands. 'Ven conmigo' is invitation and command merged. The informality creates closeness. Use with friends, family, children. The brevity feels natural in speech. These short commands are deeply embedded in spoken Spanish. They sound right to native ears.

items.question: _____ amables. (ustedes)
items.translation: (Be kind - you all formal)
items.explanation: Ustedes commands use subjunctive plural. 'Ser' subjunctive: 'sean'. This addresses multiple people formally or Latin American informal. 'Sean amables' requests kindness from a group. Teacher to students, boss to team. The plural subjunctive form works for formal or informal plural outside Spain. 'Sean' is respectfully directive to a group. It maintains politeness while being clear.

items.question: No _____ miedo. (tú)
items.translation: (Don't be afraid)
items.explanation: Negative tú command: subjunctive. 'No tengas miedo' is encouraging prohibition. You're telling someone not to fear. The subjunctive gives it gentle firmness. Negative commands use subjunctive to soften the prohibition. 'No tengas' is more gentle than a stark 'no'. The mood adds nuance. This is comforting language, encouraging bravery.

items.question: _____ cuidado. (usted)
items.translation: (Be careful - formal)
items.explanation: 'Tener' formal command: 'tenga'. Warning or advice in formal register. 'Tenga cuidado' is standard cautionary phrase. Very common in Spanish. Warning signs, formal warnings use this. The formality shows concern without presumption. 'Tenga cuidado' respects the listener while warning them. Professional caution expressed formally. The command is clear but courteous.

items.question: _____ la mano. (vosotros)
items.translation: (Raise your hand - you all)
items.explanation: Vosotros command: 'levantar' minus -r plus -d = 'levantad'. Regular formation. 'Levantad la mano' is classroom language in Spain. Teacher addressing students. The plural informal feels warm and inclusive. Vosotros commands are for your group. Spain's informal plural maintains this distinction. The -d ending is distinctive and easy to form.

items.question: No _____ tiempo. (ustedes)
items.translation: (Don't waste time - you all)
items.explanation: Negative ustedes command: plural subjunctive. 'No pierdan tiempo' addresses group formally or Latin American informal. The prohibition is clear and applies to all. Plural subjunctive for negative commands maintains the pattern. All negatives use subjunctive mood. 'No pierdan' is firm group directive. The plural makes it collective responsibility. Time is valuable, don't waste it.

name: Conditional Sentences
description: Master si clauses and conditional structures

items.question: Si _____ tiempo, te _____.
items.translation: (If I have time, I will call you)
items.explanation: Real future conditions use present tense after 'si' + future tense in result clause. Never use future after 'si' for real conditions.

items.question: Si _____ rico, _____ por el mundo.
items.translation: (If I were rich, I would travel the world)
items.explanation: Hypothetical conditions use imperfect subjunctive + conditional. This expresses imaginary situations contrary to current reality.

items.question: Si _____ mañana, nos _____ en casa.
items.translation: (If it rains tomorrow, we will stay home)
items.explanation: Even with future time words, likely conditions use present indicative after 'si'. Spanish treats real future possibilities as extensions of present reality.

items.question: Si hubieras estudiado, _____ aprobado.
items.translation: (If you had studied, you would have passed)
items.explanation: Past contrary-to-fact uses pluperfect subjunctive + conditional perfect. This expresses regret about missed past opportunities.

items.question: _____ si _____.
items.translation: (I would go if I could)
items.explanation: Conditional + imperfect subjunctive for hypothetical present situations. Word order is flexible in Spanish.

items.question: Si ella _____, dile que estoy ocupado.
items.translation: (If she calls, tell her I'm busy)
items.explanation: Real conditions with imperative result use present indicative after 'si'. Commands in result clauses are common for conditional instructions.

items.question: Si hubiera sabido, no _____ venido.
items.translation: (If I had known, I wouldn't have come)
items.explanation: Pluperfect subjunctive + conditional perfect expresses past regrets. Both parts remain in perfect forms for completed past actions.

items.question: Si la _____, salúdala de mi parte.
items.translation: (If you see her, say hello for me)
items.explanation: Present indicative after 'si' for real future possibilities. Never use future tense after 'si' in real conditions.

items.question: _____ una casa si _____ dinero.
items.translation: (I would buy a house if I had money)
items.explanation: Conditional + imperfect subjunctive for hypothetical present. This clearly distinguishes imaginary from real future possibilities.

items.question: Si _____ ahora, _____ a tiempo.
items.translation: (If we leave now, we will arrive on time)
items.explanation: Present + future for real immediate future conditions. The 'ahora' doesn't change the rule of using present after 'si'.

items.question: Si yo _____ tú, _____ la oferta.
items.translation: (If I were you, I would accept the offer)
items.explanation: Classic advice structure using imperfect subjunctive + conditional. This is impossible (you can't be someone else) so it requires subjunctive.

items.question: Si hubieran llegado antes, lo _____ visto.
items.translation: (If they had arrived earlier, they would have seen him)
items.explanation: Pluperfect subjunctive + conditional perfect for past contrary-to-fact. This expresses missed opportunities in the past.

items.question: Si _____ a diario, _____.
items.translation: (If you practice daily, you will improve)
items.explanation: Present + future for likely, realistic conditions. This expresses confident cause and effect.

items.question: Ella _____ ayudado si hubiera sabido.
items.translation: (She would have helped if she had known)
items.explanation: Conditional perfect + pluperfect subjunctive for past hypotheticals. Spanish allows flexible word order for si clauses.

items.question: Si _____ frío, _____ una chaqueta.
items.translation: (If it's cold, I'll wear a jacket)
items.explanation: Present + future for real weather conditions. Spanish uses 'hacer' for weather expressions, not 'ser' or 'estar'.

name: Passive Voice
description: Learn passive constructions with ser and se

items.question: La casa _____ construida en 1990.
items.translation: (The house was built in 1990)
items.explanation: Passive voice uses 'ser' + past participle agreeing in gender/number. Use preterite 'fue' for completed past actions with specific time.

items.question: _____ habla español aquí.
items.translation: (Spanish is spoken here)
items.explanation: Reflexive passive 'se' + verb is more natural than 'ser' passive for general statements. This construction is the preferred Spanish way.

items.question: Los libros _____ escritos por García Márquez.
items.translation: (The books were written by García Márquez)
items.explanation: When mentioning the agent (by whom), use 'ser' passive not reflexive 'se'. The 'por' phrase requires this structure.

items.question: La puerta _____ abre a las 9.
items.translation: (The door is opened at 9 AM)
items.explanation: Habitual actions prefer reflexive passive over 'ser' construction. This describes regular daily routines.

items.question: La carta _____ enviada mañana.
items.translation: (The letter will be sent tomorrow)
items.explanation: Future passive uses future of 'ser' + past participle. Alternative 'se enviará' is also correct and perhaps more natural.

items.question: Esas casas _____ vendidas el año pasado.
items.translation: (Those houses were sold last year)
items.explanation: Preterite 'ser' + past participle for completed past actions. Specific time markers like 'el año pasado' make preterite the natural choice.

items.question: _____ produce vino en esta región.
items.translation: (Wine is produced in this region)
items.explanation: General facts strongly prefer reflexive passive. This is the natural Spanish way for stating general truths.

items.question: El coche _____ sido reparado.
items.translation: (The car has been repaired)
items.explanation: Present perfect passive needs present perfect of 'ser' + past participle. Formula: haber + sido + past participle.

items.question: _____ hablan muchos idiomas en Nueva York.
items.translation: (Many languages are spoken in New York)
items.explanation: Reflexive passive with plural verb for plural object. Use 'se hablan' (plural) because 'idiomas' is plural.

items.question: Las ventanas _____ rotas durante la tormenta.
items.translation: (The windows were broken during the storm)
items.explanation: Preterite passive emphasizes the specific past event. Alternative 'se rompieron' works but sounds less formal.

items.question: _____ enseña inglés en todas las escuelas.
items.translation: (English is taught in all schools)
items.explanation: Habitual general actions strongly prefer reflexive passive. This is fundamental Spanish idiom for repeated facts.

items.question: La película _____ dirigida por Almodóvar.
items.translation: (The movie was directed by Almodóvar)
items.explanation: Use 'ser' passive when crediting the agent with 'por' phrase. You can't use reflexive 'se' when mentioning who did it.

items.question: Estas camisas _____ hacen en España.
items.translation: (These shirts are made in Spain)
items.explanation: Manufacturing and origin descriptions use reflexive passive. This is standard for describing where products are made.

items.question: El problema _____ resuelto pronto.
items.translation: (The problem will be solved soon)
items.explanation: Future 'ser' + past participle for future passive. Alternative 'se resolverá' sounds more casual.

items.question: _____ cultiva café en Colombia.
items.translation: (Coffee is grown in Colombia)
items.explanation: Products and origins use reflexive passive. This construction dominates agricultural and production contexts.

name: Subjunctive in Noun Clauses
description: Master when to use subjunctive after main verbs

items.question: Quiero que _____ conmigo.
items.translation: (I want you to come with me)
items.explanation: Verbs of wanting trigger subjunctive when subject changes. 'Querer que' always requires subjunctive in the dependent clause.

items.question: Es importante que _____.
items.translation: (It's important that they understand)
items.explanation: Impersonal expressions of importance require subjunctive. These express opinions about what should happen, not facts.

items.question: Dudo que _____ la respuesta.
items.translation: (I doubt that he knows the answer)
items.explanation: Doubt expressions trigger subjunctive mood. When you doubt something, you're treating it as uncertain or unreal.

items.question: Ella espera que _____ temprano.
items.translation: (She hopes that we arrive early)
items.explanation: Hope about others' actions triggers subjunctive. Hoping expresses desired future, not guaranteed reality.

items.question: Es necesario que _____ más.
items.translation: (It's necessary that you study more)
items.explanation: Necessity and obligation trigger subjunctive. This expresses what should happen, not what is happening.

items.question: Recomiendo que _____ a un médico.
items.translation: (I recommend that she see a doctor)
items.explanation: Recommendation verbs require subjunctive. These express advice about uncertain future actions.

items.question: Temo que _____.
items.translation: (I'm afraid that it will rain)
items.explanation: Emotions about uncertain events trigger subjunctive. Fear deals with events that might happen, not certainties.

items.question: Es bueno que _____ aquí.
items.translation: (It's good that you're here)
items.explanation: Value judgments require subjunctive even when fact is true. Spanish distinguishes objective truth from subjective evaluation.

items.question: Prefiero que no _____.
items.translation: (I prefer that they not come)
items.explanation: Preference verbs trigger subjunctive. Preferences express desires about uncertain events, not current reality.

items.question: Es posible que _____ razón.
items.translation: (It's possible that he's right)
items.explanation: Possibility expressions trigger subjunctive. Degree of certainty determines mood: possible = subjunctive, certain = indicative.

items.question: Me alegro de que _____.
items.translation: (I'm happy that you're coming)
items.explanation: Emotion verbs about future/uncertain events trigger subjunctive. Spanish uses subjunctive for emotions about non-facts.

items.question: Es extraño que no _____.
items.translation: (It's strange that she doesn't answer)
items.explanation: Strangeness and surprise expressions trigger subjunctive. Subjective reactions to situations need subjunctive mood.

items.question: Piden que _____ callados.
items.translation: (They ask that we be quiet)
items.explanation: Request verbs trigger subjunctive. Commands embedded in 'que' clauses always use subjunctive mood.

items.question: No creo que _____ en casa.
items.translation: (I don't think she's at home)
items.explanation: Negative opinion verbs trigger subjunctive. 'Creo que está' uses indicative, but 'no creo que esté' uses subjunctive.

items.question: Es imposible que _____ hoy.
items.translation: (It's impossible that they finish today)
items.explanation: Impossibility expressions trigger subjunctive. Impossible events aren't real, perfect for subjunctive mood.

name: Commands and Imperatives
description: Learn to give orders and instructions

items.question: ¡_____ despacio!
items.translation: (Speak slowly! - informal singular)
items.explanation: Affirmative tú commands use third person singular present. Take 'él habla' form for informal commands.

items.question: ¡No _____ eso!
items.translation: (Don't eat that! - informal singular)
items.explanation: Negative tú commands use present subjunctive. All negative commands across all forms use subjunctive.

items.question: ¡_____ aquí!
items.translation: (Come here! - formal singular)
items.explanation: Usted commands always use present subjunctive for both affirmative and negative. This is simpler than tú commands.

items.question: ¡_____ cuidado!
items.translation: (Be careful! - informal singular)
items.explanation: 'Tener' has irregular tú command 'ten'. Memorize eight irregular affirmative tú commands for common verbs.

items.question: ¡No _____!
items.translation: (Don't go! - informal singular)
items.explanation: Negative commands use subjunctive even for irregular verbs. Irregularity only affects affirmative tú commands.

items.question: ¡_____ la verdad!
items.translation: (Tell me the truth! - informal singular)
items.explanation: Irregular 'di' + attached pronoun 'me' = 'Dime'. Pronouns always attach to affirmative commands.

items.question: ¡_____ a la playa!
items.translation: (Let's go to the beach!)
items.explanation: For 'let's go' specifically, use 'vamos' not subjunctive 'vayamos'. This is an exception to the normal pattern.

items.question: ¡_____ ahora!
items.translation: (Do it now! - formal singular)
items.explanation: Usted command 'haga' + attached pronoun 'lo' = 'Hágalo'. Accent maintains stress when pronoun attaches.

items.question: ¡No _____ miedo!
items.translation: (Don't be afraid! - informal singular)
items.explanation: Negative tú command uses regular subjunctive 'tengas'. Even irregular verbs follow negative = subjunctive rule.

items.question: ¡_____ aquí!
items.translation: (Put it here! - informal singular)
items.explanation: Irregular command 'pon' + attached 'lo' = 'Ponlo'. No accent needed for monosyllables.

items.question: ¡No _____ eso!
items.translation: (Don't do that! - formal singular)
items.explanation: Negative usted command uses subjunctive. Formal commands are consistent: both affirmative and negative use subjunctive.

items.translation: (Write to me! - informal plural Spain)
items.explanation: Vosotros affirmative: infinitive minus -r plus -d. This form is mainly used in Spain.

items.translation: (Listen! - formal plural)
items.explanation: Ustedes command uses subjunctive third person plural. This works for all regions and formal plural contexts.

items.question: ¡No _____!
items.translation: (Don't forget! - informal singular)
items.explanation: Negative tú command uses present subjunctive. This is the consistent pattern for all negative commands.

items.translation: (Get out! - informal singular)
items.explanation: 'Salir' has irregular tú command 'sal'. This is one of eight essential irregular commands to memorize.

items.question: Ella es _____ alta _____ yo.
items.translation: (She is taller than me)
items.explanation: Superiority comparisons use 'más...que' structure. This works for all regular comparisons in Spanish.

items.question: Este libro es _____ que ese.
items.translation: (This book is better than that one)
items.explanation: 'Bueno' has irregular comparative 'mejor'. Never say 'más bueno' for better.

items.question: Soy _____ alto _____ mi hermano.
items.translation: (I am as tall as my brother)
items.explanation: Equal comparisons use 'tan...como' structure. This is different from English using 'as' twice.

items.question: Él es _____ alto _____ la clase.
items.translation: (He is the tallest in the class)
items.explanation: Superlatives use 'el más' + adjective + 'de'. Always use 'de', never 'en' for location.

items.question: Ella corre _____ rápido _____ él.
items.translation: (She runs faster than him)
items.explanation: Comparatives work the same with adverbs as adjectives. Spanish uses 'más' consistently without adding -er.

items.question: Esta es _____ película que he visto.
items.translation: (This is the worst movie I've seen)
items.explanation: 'Malo' has irregular comparative/superlative 'peor'. Like 'mejor', never use 'más malo'.

items.question: Tengo _____ dinero _____ tú.
items.translation: (I have less money than you)
items.explanation: Inferiority comparisons use 'menos...que'. This is the mirror structure of 'más...que'.

items.question: Esta casa es _____ vieja _____ la mía.
items.translation: (This house is older than mine)
items.explanation: Standard 'más...que' for age comparisons. Possessive 'mía' requires article: 'la mía'.

items.question: Ella es _____ de la familia.
items.translation: (She is the youngest of the family)
items.explanation: 'Menor' is standard for youngest in families. It's more traditional than 'más joven'.

items.question: Tengo _____ libros _____ tú.
items.translation: (I have as many books as you)
items.explanation: Equal quantities use 'tanto/a/os/as...como' agreeing with noun. Use 'tantos' for masculine plural 'libros'.

items.question: Él es _____ jugador del equipo.
items.translation: (He is the best player on the team)
items.explanation: Superlative of 'bueno' is 'el mejor'. Article + irregular comparative creates superlative.

items.question: Este ejercicio es _____ fácil _____ el anterior.
items.translation: (This exercise is easier than the previous one)
items.explanation: Standard superiority comparison with 'más...que'. Spanish uses this consistently across all adjectives.

items.question: Ella trabaja _____ duro _____ su colega.
items.translation: (She works as hard as her colleague)
items.explanation: Equal intensity with adverbs uses 'tan...como'. This parallels adjective equal comparisons.

items.question: Este es el restaurante _____ caro _____ la ciudad.
items.translation: (This is the most expensive restaurant in the city)
items.explanation: Superlatives always use 'de' for location or group. English 'in' translates to Spanish 'de'.

items.question: Soy _____ que mi hermana.
items.translation: (I am older than my sister)
items.explanation: 'Mayor' is standard for older in age comparisons. It's more natural than 'más viejo' for people.

name: Object Pronouns
description: Master direct and indirect object pronouns

items.question: _____ veo todos los días.
items.translation: (I see her every day)
items.explanation: Direct object pronoun for feminine singular is 'la'. Gender matters for direct objects.

items.question: Ella _____ da el libro.
items.translation: (She gives him the book)
items.explanation: Indirect object pronoun 'le' means 'to him/her'. Context clarifies gender.

items.question: _____ voy a llamar mañana.
items.translation: (I'm going to call you tomorrow - informal)
items.explanation: Direct object pronoun for informal 'you' is 'te'. It serves both direct and indirect object.

items.question: Ella _____ _____ dio.
items.translation: (She gave it to me - the book masculine)
items.explanation: Indirect before direct always. 'Me' (to me) + 'lo' (it masculine) = 'me lo'.

items.question: _____ vemos cada semana.
items.translation: (We see them every week - masculine)
items.explanation: Direct object pronoun for masculine plural is 'los'. Use for all-male or mixed groups.

items.question: _____ _____ está explicando.
items.translation: (He's explaining it to us - the lesson feminine)
items.explanation: Indirect 'nos' before direct 'la'. Lesson is feminine, so use 'la'.

items.question: ¡No _____ _____ digas!
items.translation: (Don't tell it to them! - the secret masculine)
items.explanation: 'Les' changes to 'se' before 'lo/la/los/las'. This prevents awkward 'le lo' combinations.

items.question: Quiero _____.
items.translation: (I want to buy them - the shoes masculine)
items.explanation: Pronouns attach to infinitives. 'Los' for masculine plural shoes.

items.question: Ella _____ manda cartas.
items.translation: (She sends us letters)
items.explanation: Indirect object pronoun for 'us' is 'nos'. It shows who receives the action.

items.question: Voy a _____.
items.translation: (I'm going to give it to her - the gift masculine)
items.explanation: Both pronouns attach to infinitive as one word: 'se' (to her) + 'lo' (it) = 'dárselo'. Accent maintains stress.

items.question: _____ _____ están trayendo.
items.translation: (They're bringing it to me - the food feminine)
items.explanation: Indirect 'me' before direct 'la'. Food is feminine, so use 'la'.

items.translation: (Tell me! - formal)
items.explanation: Pronouns attach to affirmative commands. Usted command 'diga' + 'me' = 'Dígame'.

items.question: _____ _____ está mostrando.
items.translation: (She's showing them to us - the photos feminine)
items.explanation: Indirect 'nos' before direct 'las'. Photos are feminine plural.

items.question: _____ escribo cada mes.
items.translation: (I write to them every month)
items.explanation: Indirect object pronoun 'les' means 'to them'. Gender doesn't matter for indirect objects.

items.question: ¡No _____ _____ des!
items.translation: (Don't give it to him! - the money masculine)
items.explanation: 'Le' becomes 'se' before 'lo'. Pronouns go before negative commands.

name: Por vs Para
description: Learn when to use por and para correctly

items.question: Este regalo es _____ ti.
items.translation: (This gift is for you)
items.explanation: 'Para' indicates recipient or destination. Use para for intended recipients of gifts.

items.question: Caminamos _____ el parque.
items.translation: (We walked through the park)
items.explanation: 'Por' indicates movement through or along a place. It describes the path of movement.

items.question: Estudio _____ ser médico.
items.translation: (I'm studying to be a doctor)
items.explanation: 'Para' expresses purpose or goal. Use para with infinitive to show purpose.

items.question: Gracias _____ todo.
items.translation: (Thanks for everything)
items.explanation: 'Por' expresses cause or reason. 'Gracias por' is always used, never 'gracias para'.

items.question: Trabajo _____ una empresa grande.
items.translation: (I work for a large company)
items.explanation: 'Para' indicates employment relationship. Use para when stating your employer.

items.question: Lucharon _____ la libertad.
items.translation: (They fought for freedom)
items.explanation: 'Por' expresses motivation or fighting for a cause. It shows the motivating force behind action.

items.question: Lo necesito _____ mañana.
items.translation: (I need it for tomorrow)
items.explanation: 'Para' indicates deadline or specific time. Use para when something is due by a certain time.

items.question: Lo hizo _____ amor.
items.translation: (He did it for love)
items.explanation: 'Por' indicates motive or reason. It shows the cause that motivated the action.

items.question: Salimos _____ Madrid.
items.translation: (We're leaving for Madrid)
items.explanation: 'Para' indicates destination with departure verbs. Use para when heading toward a place.

items.question: Pagué $20 _____ esta camisa.
items.translation: (I paid $20 for this shirt)
items.explanation: 'Por' is used for exchanges and prices. It marks what you give to get something.

items.question: Ella estudia tres horas _____ día.
items.translation: (She studies three hours per day)
items.explanation: 'Por' means 'per' in rates and frequencies. English 'per' equals Spanish 'por'.

items.question: _____ un niño, es muy maduro.
items.translation: (For a child, he's very mature)
items.explanation: 'Para' in comparisons means 'considering that'. It frames opinions relative to circumstances.

items.question: Te llamé _____ teléfono.
items.translation: (I called you on the phone)
items.explanation: 'Por' indicates means or method. English 'by' or 'via' equals Spanish 'por'.

items.question: Este libro es _____ aprender español.
items.translation: (This book is to learn Spanish)
items.explanation: 'Para' with infinitive shows purpose. Use para to explain what something is for.

items.question: Fue arrestado _____ robo.
items.translation: (He was arrested for theft)
items.explanation: 'Por' indicates reason for something happening. Use por for cause, not purpose.

items.question: _____ despierto a las 7.
items.translation: (I wake up at 7 AM)
items.explanation: Reflexive verbs use reflexive pronouns showing action done to oneself. 'Despertarse' needs 'me' for first person.

items.question: Ella _____ está vistiendo.
items.translation: (She is getting dressed)
items.explanation: Progressive tenses keep reflexive pronoun before conjugated auxiliary. Alternative: 'está vistiéndose'.

items.question: _____ sentamos aquí.
items.translation: (We sit down here)
items.explanation: First person plural reflexive pronoun is 'nos'. 'Sentarse' requires reflexive for 'sit down'.

items.question: ¡No _____ preocupes!
items.translation: (Don't worry! - informal singular)
items.explanation: Negative tú command with reflexive pronoun. Reflexive 'te' goes before negative command.

items.question: _____ lavan las manos.
items.translation: (They wash their hands)
items.explanation: Reflexive pronouns with body parts - no possessive needed! Use reflexive 'se' plus article 'las'.

items.question: _____ voy a acostar temprano.
items.translation: (I'm going to go to bed early)
items.explanation: With ir + a + infinitive, reflexive pronoun goes before conjugated verb. Alternative: 'voy a acostarme'.

items.question: _____ durmió rápidamente.
items.translation: (She fell asleep quickly)
items.explanation: 'Dormirse' (fall asleep) is reflexive, different from 'dormir' (sleep). Reflexive changes the meaning.

items.question: _____ casamos el año pasado.
items.translation: (We got married last year)
items.explanation: 'Casarse' is reciprocal reflexive meaning 'married each other'. 'Nos' shows mutual action.

items.question: _____ afeita cada mañana.
items.translation: (He shaves every morning)
items.explanation: 'Afeitarse' (shave oneself) requires reflexive. Daily grooming verbs are typically reflexive in Spanish.

items.question: _____ estoy divirtiendo en la fiesta.
items.translation: (I'm having fun at the party)
items.explanation: 'Divertirse' (enjoy oneself) is reflexive. Progressive form puts reflexive before 'estoy'.

items.question: _____ enojaron.
items.translation: (They got angry)
items.explanation: 'Enojarse' (get angry) is reflexive showing emotional state change. Emotion verbs use reflexive structure.

items.translation: (Remember! - informal singular)
items.explanation: Affirmative tú command with attached reflexive. Command 'acuerda' + 'te' = 'acuérdate' with accent.

items.question: _____ cepillo los dientes dos veces al día.
items.translation: (I brush my teeth twice a day)
items.explanation: Reflexive with body parts uses reflexive pronoun and article, not possessive. 'Me' makes clear they're your teeth.

items.question: _____ vamos a encontrar a las 5.
items.translation: (We're going to meet at 5 PM)
items.explanation: 'Encontrarse' (meet each other) is reciprocal reflexive. 'Nos' shows mutual meeting action.

items.question: Ella _____ siente feliz hoy.
items.translation: (She feels happy today)
items.explanation: 'Sentirse' (feel emotion/state) is reflexive. Different from 'sentir' which means perceive externally.

name: Past Subjunctive
description: Learn the imperfect subjunctive forms and uses

items.question: Deseaba que _____ aquí.
items.translation: (I wished you were here)
items.explanation: Past tense main verb triggers imperfect subjunctive in dependent clause. Past desire requires past subjunctive form.

items.question: Ella dudaba que él _____.
items.translation: (She doubted that he would come)
items.explanation: Doubt in past uses imperfect subjunctive. Past doubt + imperfect subjunctive = proper sequence of tenses.

items.question: Era importante que _____.
items.translation: (It was important that they studied)
items.explanation: Past impersonal expressions use imperfect subjunctive. The pattern holds across all similar expressions.

items.question: Quería que ella me _____.
items.translation: (I wanted her to call me)
items.explanation: Wanting in past triggers imperfect subjunctive. Past desire requires past subjunctive form.

items.question: Recomendó que _____ al médico.
items.translation: (He recommended that we go to the doctor)
items.explanation: Recommendations in past use imperfect subjunctive. 'Ir' has irregular imperfect subjunctive forms.

items.question: Buscaban a alguien que _____ francés.
items.translation: (They were looking for someone who spoke French)
items.explanation: Adjective clauses with indefinite antecedent use subjunctive. Seeking something that may not exist requires subjunctive.

items.question: Esperaba que _____.
items.translation: (I hoped that you would understand)
items.explanation: Hope in past requires imperfect subjunctive. Past hope about uncertain outcome uses past subjunctive.

items.question: Pidió que _____ temprano.
items.translation: (She asked that we arrive early)
items.explanation: Requests in past trigger imperfect subjunctive. Both preterite and imperfect main verbs trigger it.

items.question: Temía que _____ perdieran.
items.translation: (I was afraid that they would get lost)
items.explanation: Fear in past with reflexive verb uses imperfect subjunctive. Reflexive stays with verb even in subjunctive.

items.question: Era imposible que él _____.
items.translation: (It was impossible that he knew)
items.explanation: Impossibility in past triggers imperfect subjunctive. 'Saber' has irregular imperfect subjunctive 'supiera'.

items.question: Sugirieron que _____ unas vacaciones.
items.translation: (They suggested that I take a vacation)
items.explanation: Suggestion in past requires imperfect subjunctive. Both preterite and imperfect trigger it.

items.question: No creía que ella _____ razón.
items.translation: (I didn't think she was right)
items.explanation: Negative belief in past triggers imperfect subjunctive. Negative 'no creía' makes it subjunctive.

items.question: Quería que lo _____.
items.translation: (He wanted us to help him)
items.explanation: Past wanting requires imperfect subjunctive. Accent on 'á' distinguishes first person plural.

items.question: Era necesario que _____ inmediatamente.
items.translation: (It was necessary that they leave immediately)
items.explanation: Past necessity requires imperfect subjunctive. The subjunctive mood persists into past tense.

items.question: Prefería que no _____ nada.
items.translation: (She preferred that we not say anything)
items.explanation: Past preference uses imperfect subjunctive. 'Decir' has irregular imperfect subjunctive stem.

items.question: _____ visitado España dos veces.
items.translation: (I have visited Spain twice)
items.explanation: Present perfect uses present of 'haber' + past participle. Use for life experiences up to now.

items.question: Ella ya _____ salido cuando llegué.
items.translation: (She had already left when I arrived)
items.explanation: Pluperfect uses imperfect of 'haber' + past participle. Shows action completed before another past action.

items.question: Para mañana, _____ terminado el proyecto.
items.translation: (By tomorrow, I will have finished the project)
items.explanation: Future perfect uses future of 'haber' + past participle. Shows completion by specific future time.

items.question: ¿_____ estado alguna vez en México?
items.translation: (Have you ever been to Mexico?)
items.explanation: Present perfect for life experiences. Use with 'alguna vez' (ever) for asking about experiences.

items.question: No _____ habían visto en años.
items.translation: (They hadn't seen each other in years)
items.explanation: Reflexive reciprocal verb in pluperfect maintains reflexive pronoun. 'Se' goes before 'habían'.

items.question: Dudo que _____ llegado todavía.
items.translation: (I doubt that he has arrived yet)
items.explanation: Present perfect subjunctive uses present subjunctive of 'haber' + past participle. Doubt triggers subjunctive.

items.question: _____ vivido aquí por cinco años.
items.translation: (We have lived here for five years)
items.explanation: Present perfect with duration shows time continuing to present. Emphasizes the continuing nature.

items.question: Nunca _____ probado sushi antes.
items.translation: (I had never tried sushi before)
items.explanation: Pluperfect with 'nunca' before a past reference point. Shows lack of experience before past moment.

items.question: Para la semana que viene, _____ habrán mudado.
items.translation: (By next week, they will have moved)
items.explanation: Reflexive verb in future perfect keeps reflexive pronoun. 'Se' goes before 'habrán'.

items.question: _____ estudiado toda la mañana.
items.translation: (She has studied all morning)
items.explanation: Present perfect for recent completed action still relevant now. Emphasizes connection to present moment.

items.question: Me alegro de que _____ venido.
items.translation: (I'm glad you have come)
items.explanation: Present perfect subjunctive after emotion in present. 'Hayas' is subjunctive of haber.

items.question: Ya _____ comido cuando lo invitamos.
items.translation: (He had already eaten when we invited him)
items.explanation: Pluperfect for action completed before another past action. Eating happened before inviting.

items.question: Esta es la mejor película que _____ visto.
items.translation: (This is the best movie I have seen)
items.explanation: Present perfect with superlative for life experiences. Keeps experiences open to future.

items.question: _____ ido si hubiera sabido.
items.translation: (I would have gone if I had known)
