items.explanation: Conditional perfect for past hypotheticals. Conditional of 'haber' + past participle.

items.question: Para cuando llegues, ya _____ salido.
items.translation: (By the time you arrive, I will have left)
items.explanation: Future perfect for action completed before future moment. Note subjunctive 'llegues' in time clause.

name: Relative Pronouns
description: Master que, quien, cual and their uses

items.question: El libro _____ leí es interesante.
items.translation: (The book that I read is interesting)
items.explanation: 'Que' is the most common relative pronoun for things. It means 'that', 'which', or 'who'.

items.question: La mujer _____ hablé es mi profesora.
items.translation: (The woman to whom I spoke is my teacher)
items.explanation: After prepositions referring to people, use 'quien'. 'Con quien' means 'with whom'.

items.question: No entiendo _____ dices.
items.translation: (I don't understand what you're saying)
items.explanation: 'Lo que' means 'what' (the thing that). Use for abstract or unknown things.

items.question: El restaurante en _____ comimos era caro.
items.translation: (The restaurant in which we ate was expensive)
items.explanation: After prepositions with things, use 'el que/la que/los que/las que' with article. Agrees with antecedent.

items.question: Las personas _____ trabajan aquí son amables.
items.translation: (The people who work here are friendly)
items.explanation: 'Que' works for both people and things as subject. Simplest and most common relative pronoun.

items.question: Ese es el problema del _____ te hablé.
items.translation: (That's the problem about which I told you)
items.explanation: After preposition + article (del = de + el), use just 'que'. The article is separate.

items.question: Hay _____ quieras, puedes pedirlo.
items.translation: (Whatever you want, you can ask for it)
items.explanation: 'Lo que' means 'what/whatever' for indefinite things. Very common in Spanish.

items.question: La chica _____ padre es médico estudia aquí.
items.translation: (The girl whose father is a doctor studies here)
items.explanation: 'Cuyo/a/os/as' means 'whose' and agrees with the thing possessed. Here 'cuyo' agrees with 'padre'.

items.question: Conocí a un hombre _____ vivió en España.
items.translation: (I met a man who lived in Spain)
items.explanation: 'Que' is standard for people in restrictive clauses. Simplest form for 'who'.

items.question: La razón por _____ vine es importante.
items.translation: (The reason for which I came is important)
items.explanation: After 'por' with abstract noun, use 'la que' with feminine article. Agrees with 'razón'.

items.question: _____ más me gusta es el chocolate.
items.translation: (What I like most is chocolate)
items.explanation: 'Lo que' for 'what' at sentence start. Common pattern: 'lo que' + verb = what (I do).

items.question: Los amigos con _____ viajé son divertidos.
items.translation: (The friends with whom I traveled are fun)
items.explanation: After prepositions, use 'quienes' (plural) for people. 'Con quienes' means 'with whom'.

items.question: La casa _____ ventanas son grandes es mía.
items.translation: (The house whose windows are big is mine)
items.explanation: 'Cuyas' (feminine plural) agrees with 'ventanas'. Possession relative pronoun agrees with thing possessed.

items.question: Eso es _____ te dije ayer.
items.translation: (That's what I told you yesterday)
items.explanation: 'Lo que' for 'what' referring to abstract ideas or statements. Very common construction.

items.question: El hotel en _____ nos quedamos era lujoso.
items.translation: (The hotel in which we stayed was luxurious)
items.explanation: After 'en' with things, use 'el que' with article. Article agrees with 'hotel' (masculine).

name: Gustar and Similar Verbs
description: Learn verbs like gustar that work backwards

items.question: _____ gusta el chocolate.
items.translation: (I like chocolate)
items.explanation: 'Gustar' works backwards: thing pleases me. 'Me gusta' literally means 'it pleases me'.

items.question: _____ gustan los deportes.
items.translation: (We like sports)
items.explanation: Plural 'gustan' because 'deportes' is plural. The thing doing the pleasing determines verb number.

items.question: A María _____ encanta bailar.
items.translation: (María loves to dance)
items.explanation: 'Encantar' works like gustar. Use indirect object pronoun 'le' for third person singular.

items.question: _____ duele la cabeza.
items.translation: (My head hurts)
items.explanation: 'Doler' (hurt) works like gustar. The body part is subject; person is indirect object.

items.question: ¿_____ interesa la política?
items.translation: (Are you interested in politics?)
items.explanation: 'Interesar' follows gustar pattern. Use indirect object pronoun before verb.

items.question: A nosotros no _____ gusta el café.
items.translation: (We don't like coffee)
items.explanation: Even with 'a nosotros' for emphasis, still use pronoun 'nos'. Both are required.

items.question: _____ quedan dos horas.
items.translation: (I have two hours left)
items.explanation: 'Quedar' (remain) works like gustar. The remaining thing is subject.

items.question: _____ falta dinero.
items.translation: (We lack money)
items.explanation: 'Faltar' (lack) follows gustar pattern. What's lacking is the subject.

items.question: A ellos _____ parecen interesantes las clases.
items.translation: (The classes seem interesting to them)
items.explanation: 'Parecer' works like gustar. Plural 'parecen' agrees with 'clases', use 'les' for them.

items.question: _____ molesta el ruido.
items.translation: (The noise bothers you - informal)
items.explanation: 'Molestar' (bother) follows gustar pattern. The annoying thing is the subject.

items.question: ¿_____ importa esto?
items.translation: (Does this matter to you?)
items.explanation: 'Importar' (matter) works like gustar. Use indirect object pronoun.

items.question: _____ fascina la música clásica.
items.translation: (Classical music fascinates me)
items.explanation: 'Fascinar' follows gustar pattern. Music is subject, 'me' is indirect object.

items.question: _____ quedan bien esos zapatos.
items.translation: (Those shoes fit you well)
items.explanation: 'Quedar' (fit) works like gustar. Plural 'quedan' because 'zapatos' is plural.

items.question: _____ hace falta más tiempo.
items.translation: (We need more time)
items.explanation: 'Hacer falta' (need) follows gustar pattern. What's needed is the subject.

items.question: A mi hermano _____ encanta cocinar.
items.translation: (My brother loves to cook)
items.explanation: 'Encantar' with infinitive takes singular verb. Use 'le' for third person singular.

name: Adverb Formation
description: Learn to form and use adverbs correctly

items.question: Habla _____.
items.translation: (He speaks slowly)
items.explanation: Form adverbs by adding -mente to feminine adjective. 'Lenta' + 'mente' = 'lentamente'.

items.question: Trabajamos _____.
items.translation: (We work hard)
items.explanation: Some adjectives function as adverbs without change. 'Duro' means both 'hard' (adjective) and 'hard' (adverb).

items.question: Canta _____.
items.translation: (She sings beautifully)
items.explanation: Feminine adjective 'bella' + -mente = 'bellamente'. Standard adverb formation.

items.question: Lo hizo _____.
items.translation: (He did it quickly)
items.explanation: 'Rápida' (feminine) + -mente = 'rápidamente'. Both this and 'rápido' are used.

items.question: Conduce _____.
items.translation: (She drives carefully)
items.explanation: 'Cuidadosa' + -mente = 'cuidadosamente'. Long but correct adverb form.

items.question: Hablan español _____.
items.translation: (They speak Spanish fluently)
items.explanation: 'Fluida' + -mente = 'fluidamente'. Alternative: 'con fluidez' (with fluency).

items.question: _____ no tengo tiempo.
items.translation: (Unfortunately I don't have time)
items.explanation: Long adjectives still add -mente. 'Desafortunada' + -mente = 'desafortunadamente'.

items.question: Llegó _____.
items.translation: (He arrived late)
items.explanation: 'Tarde' (late) is already an adverb. No need to add -mente.

items.question: Vive muy _____.
items.translation: (She lives very happily)
items.explanation: 'Feliz' becomes 'felizmente'. Note 'z' changes to 'c' before adding -mente.

items.question: Escribes _____.
items.translation: (You write clearly)
items.explanation: 'Clara' + -mente = 'claramente'. Standard adverb formation from feminine form.

items.question: Resolvió el problema _____.
items.translation: (He solved the problem easily)
items.explanation: 'Fácil' + -mente = 'fácilmente'. Accent remains on original syllable.

items.question: Trabaja _____ y _____.
items.translation: (She works efficiently and quickly)
items.explanation: With multiple -mente adverbs, only last one takes -mente. First stays in feminine adjective form.

items.question: Habla _____.
items.translation: (He speaks sincerely)
items.explanation: 'Sincera' + -mente = 'sinceramente'. Regular formation pattern.

items.question: Viene aquí _____.
items.translation: (She comes here frequently)
items.explanation: 'Frecuente' + -mente = 'frecuentemente'. Alternative: 'con frecuencia'.

items.question: Actuó _____.
items.translation: (He acted bravely)
items.explanation: 'Valiente' + -mente = 'valientemente'. Adjectives ending in -e add -mente directly.

name: Indefinite Expressions
description: Master algo, alguien, alguno and negatives

items.question: No veo a _____.
items.translation: (I don't see anyone)
items.explanation: 'Nadie' means 'no one/nobody'. After negative verb, use negative words.

items.question: ¿Hay _____ en casa?
items.translation: (Is there someone at home?)
items.explanation: 'Alguien' means 'someone/somebody'. Use in questions or affirmative statements.

items.question: No tengo _____ que hacer.
items.translation: (I have nothing to do)
items.explanation: 'Nada' means 'nothing'. After negative verb, use negative indefinites.

items.question: ¿Quieres _____ de beber?
items.translation: (Do you want something to drink?)
items.explanation: 'Algo' means 'something'. Use in questions and affirmative contexts.

items.question: No vino _____ estudiante.
items.translation: (No student came)
items.explanation: 'Ningún' (no/not any) before masculine singular noun. Shortened from 'ninguno'.

items.question: _____ vez fuiste a París?
items.translation: (Have you ever been to Paris?)
items.explanation: 'Alguna vez' means 'ever/sometime'. Use affirmative form in questions.

items.question: No quiero _____ café.
items.translation: (I don't want any coffee)
items.explanation: After negative, use 'ningún' before masculine nouns. Means 'no/not any'.

items.question: _____ persona puede hacerlo.
items.translation: (Any person can do it)
items.explanation: 'Cualquier' means 'any' (whichever). Used before singular nouns for 'any at all'.

items.question: _____ me llamó por teléfono.
items.translation: (Someone called me on the phone)
items.explanation: 'Alguien' as subject means 'someone'. Use in affirmative statements.

items.question: No hay _____ solución.
items.translation: (There is no solution)
items.explanation: 'Ninguna' (no/not any) before feminine nouns. After negative 'no hay', use negative form.

items.question: _____ día te visitaré.
items.translation: (Some day I'll visit you)
items.explanation: 'Algún día' means 'some day/someday'. Shortened before masculine singular nouns.

items.question: No conozco a _____ de ellos.
items.translation: (I don't know any of them)
items.explanation: 'Ninguno' means 'none/not any one'. Use after 'a' or alone without noun.

items.question: _____ cosa que necesites, dime.
items.translation: (Anything you need, tell me)
items.explanation: 'Cualquier' before nouns means 'any/whatever'. Shows complete openness.

items.question: No tenemos _____ problema.
items.translation: (We don't have any problem)
items.explanation: After negative, 'ningún' before masculine noun. Means 'no/not any'.

items.question: ¿Conoces a _____ aquí?
items.translation: (Do you know someone here?)
items.explanation: 'Alguien' in questions means 'someone/anybody'. Use affirmative form in questions.

items.question: Tengo _____ años.
items.translation: (I am twenty-one years old)
items.explanation: Numbers 21-29 are written as one word. 'Veintiuno' for twenty-one.

items.question: Hay _____ libro en la mesa.
items.translation: (There is one book on the table)
items.explanation: 'Uno' becomes 'un' before masculine nouns. Article form for counting.

items.question: Son las _____ de la tarde.
items.translation: (It's three in the afternoon)
items.explanation: Basic number 'tres' (three) doesn't change. Use with 'las' for time.

items.question: Compré _____ manzanas.
items.translation: (I bought a hundred apples)
items.explanation: 'Ciento' shortens to 'cien' before nouns. Use 'cien' directly before what you're counting.

items.question: Hay _____ personas aquí.
items.translation: (There are two hundred people here)
items.explanation: Hundreds agree in gender. 'Doscientas' for feminine 'personas'.

items.question: Es el _____ día del mes.
items.translation: (It's the first day of the month)
items.explanation: 'Primero' shortens to 'primer' before masculine singular nouns. First becomes 'primer día'.

items.question: Tiene _____ años.
items.translation: (She is thirty-one years old)
items.explanation: Numbers 31-99 use 'y' between tens and ones. Written as three words.

items.question: Cuesta _____ euros.
items.translation: (It costs a thousand euros)
items.explanation: 'Mil' (thousand) doesn't use 'un' before it. Just 'mil', not 'un mil'.

items.question: Pagué _____ dólares.
items.translation: (I paid twenty-one dollars)
items.explanation: 'Veintiuno' shortens to 'veintiún' before masculine nouns. 'Veintiún dólares'.

items.question: La _____ lección es difícil.
items.translation: (The third lesson is difficult)
items.explanation: Ordinal numbers agree in gender. 'Tercera' for feminine 'lección'.

items.question: Viven en el _____ piso.
items.translation: (They live on the fifth floor)
items.explanation: Ordinal 'quinto' (fifth) agrees with masculine 'piso'. Don't use cardinal 'cinco' for order.

items.question: Hay _____ de estudiantes.
items.translation: (There are millions of students)
items.explanation: 'Millones' (millions) is plural. Use 'millones de' before nouns.

items.question: Es la _____ vez que vengo.
items.translation: (It's the first time I come)
items.explanation: 'Primera' (first feminine) agrees with 'vez'. Full form before feminine nouns.

items.question: Tenemos _____ páginas que leer.
items.translation: (We have five hundred pages to read)
items.explanation: 'Quinientas' agrees with feminine 'páginas'. Hundreds change for gender.

items.question: Celebramos el _____ aniversario.
items.translation: (We celebrate the tenth anniversary)
items.explanation: 'Décimo' (tenth) agrees with masculine 'aniversario'. Ordinals agree in gender and number.

items.question: _____ quiero _____.
items.translation: (I don't want it)
items.explanation: Negation goes before verb; object pronouns go before conjugated verbs. 'No lo quiero'.

items.question: _____ grande es la casa.
items.translation: (How big the house is!)
items.explanation: 'Qué' + adjective in exclamations. Inversion pattern: Qué + adjective + verb + subject.

items.question: El libro _____ ayer era interesante.
items.translation: (The book I read yesterday was interesting)
items.explanation: Relative clause comes after noun. 'Que' introduces clause, normal word order follows.

items.question: _____ _____ está comiendo?
items.translation: (What is he eating?)
items.explanation: 'Qué' means 'what' for things. Question word comes first in Spanish questions.

items.question: Ayer _____ _____ al cine.
items.translation: (Yesterday I went to the movies)
items.explanation: Time expressions can come first; subject pronouns often omitted. 'Ayer fui' is natural.

items.question: _____ _____ María el libro.
items.translation: (María gave me the book)
items.explanation: Indirect object pronoun comes before verb. 'Me dio' is standard order.

items.question: Nunca _____ _____ visto.
items.translation: (I have never seen it)
items.explanation: Pronoun goes before auxiliary 'haber' in compound tenses. 'Lo he visto'.

items.question: En la mesa _____ un libro.
items.translation: (On the table there is a book)
items.explanation: Location can come first for emphasis. 'Hay' means 'there is/are'.

items.question: Muy _____ habla español.
items.translation: (He speaks Spanish very well)
items.explanation: 'Muy' modifies adverbs. 'Muy bien' (very well) modifies how he speaks.

items.question: A Juan _____ gusta el fútbol.
items.translation: (Juan likes soccer)
items.explanation: Even with 'a Juan' for emphasis, include pronoun 'le'. Both are required with gustar.

items.question: ¿_____ vive tu hermana?
items.translation: (Where does your sister live?)
items.explanation: Question word 'dónde' (where) comes first. Requires written accent in questions.

items.question: Siempre _____ temprano.
items.translation: (I always arrive early)
items.explanation: Adverbs of frequency come before verb; subject pronoun optional. 'Siempre llego'.

items.question: _____ coche es más rápido.
items.translation: (Which car is faster?)
items.explanation: 'Qué' before noun means 'which/what'. 'Qué coche' = which car.

items.question: Ya _____ _____ hecho.
items.translation: (I have already done it)
items.explanation: Adverb 'ya' can come first; pronoun before auxiliary. 'Ya lo he hecho'.

items.question: Pocas veces _____ al gimnasio.
items.translation: (I rarely go to the gym)
items.explanation: Frequency expression first; subject pronoun typically omitted. 'Pocas veces voy'.

items.question: Voy _____ la escuela.
items.translation: (I go to school)
items.explanation: Motion toward a place uses 'a'. 'Ir a' (go to) is standard pattern.

items.question: El libro está _____ la mesa.
items.translation: (The book is on the table)
items.explanation: 'En' means 'on' for surfaces and 'in' for containers. Most common location preposition.

items.question: Vengo _____ México.
items.translation: (I come from Mexico)
items.explanation: 'De' indicates origin or 'from'. 'Ser/venir de' shows where you're from.

items.question: Estudio _____ mi amigo.
items.translation: (I study with my friend)
items.explanation: 'Con' means 'with' (accompaniment). Standard for doing things together.

items.question: El gato está _____ la silla.
items.translation: (The cat is under the chair)
items.explanation: 'Debajo de' means 'under/underneath'. Compound preposition needs 'de'.

items.question: Vivo cerca _____ parque.
items.translation: (I live near the park)
items.explanation: 'Cerca de' (near) + 'el' = 'del'. Contraction is required.

items.question: Trabajo _____ las 9 _____ las 5.
items.translation: (I work from 9 to 5)
items.explanation: 'De...a' shows time range. Both 'de...a' and 'desde...hasta' work.

items.question: El coche _____ mi padre es nuevo.
items.translation: (My father's car is new)
items.explanation: 'De' shows possession. 'El coche de mi padre' = my father's car.

items.question: Está _____ la puerta.
items.translation: (It's behind the door)
items.explanation: 'Detrás de' means 'behind'. Compound preposition requiring 'de'.

items.question: Vamos _____ pie.
items.translation: (We go on foot)
items.explanation: 'A pie' is fixed expression meaning 'on foot'. Always uses 'a'.

items.question: Viajo _____ tren.
items.translation: (I travel by train)
items.explanation: Transportation uses 'en'. 'En tren/coche/avión' for vehicles.

items.question: Lo hice _____ ti.
items.translation: (I did it without you)
items.explanation: 'Sin' means 'without'. Opposite of 'con' (with).

items.question: Está _____ mi casa.
items.translation: (It's in front of my house)
items.explanation: 'Delante de' means 'in front of'. Opposite of 'detrás de' (behind).

items.question: Camino _____ la playa.
items.translation: (I walk toward the beach)
items.explanation: 'Hacia' indicates direction without implying arrival. Different from 'a' which implies destination.

items.question: Corro _____ el parque.
items.translation: (I run until the park)
items.explanation: 'Hasta' means 'until/up to' indicating endpoint. Different from 'hacia' (toward).

name: Demonstrative Adjectives & Pronouns
description: Master este, ese, aquel and their forms

items.question: _____ libro es interesante.
items.translation: (This book is interesting)
items.explanation: 'Este/esta/estos/estas' means 'this/these' (close to speaker). Agrees with noun in gender/number.

items.question: _____ casa es grande.
items.translation: (That house is big - near you)
items.explanation: 'Ese/esa/esos/esas' means 'that/those' (near listener). Feminine 'esa' for feminine 'casa'.

items.question: _____ montañas son hermosas.
items.translation: (Those mountains are beautiful - far away)
items.explanation: 'Aquel/aquella/aquellos/aquellas' for distant things. Feminine plural 'aquellas' for 'montañas'.

items.question: _____ es mi coche.
items.translation: (This is my car)
items.explanation: Use 'este' (not 'esto') when you know the noun's gender. 'Este' agrees with masculine 'coche'.

items.question: No me gusta _____.
items.translation: (I don't like that - unspecified thing)
items.explanation: 'Esto/eso/aquello' are neuter for unknown or abstract things. 'Eso' for that thing.

items.question: _____ zapatos son cómodos.
items.translation: (These shoes are comfortable)
items.explanation: 'Estos' (masculine plural) for 'these' near speaker. Agrees with masculine plural 'zapatos'.

items.question: ¿Qué es _____?
items.translation: (What is this?)
items.explanation: Neuter 'esto' when you don't know what thing is. Use before identifying it.

items.question: Prefiero _____ camisa que _____.
items.translation: (I prefer this shirt over that one)
items.explanation: 'Esta' (this) for close item, 'esa' (that) for item near listener. Both feminine for 'camisa'.

items.question: _____ edificios son muy altos.
items.translation: (Those buildings are very tall - far)
items.explanation: 'Aquellos' for distant things. Masculine plural agrees with 'edificios'.

items.question: _____ chicas son mis amigas.
items.translation: (These girls are my friends)
items.explanation: 'Estas' (feminine plural) for 'these' near speaker. Agrees with 'chicas'.

items.question: ¿Qué significa _____?
items.translation: (What does that mean? - abstract)
items.explanation: Neuter 'eso' for abstract concepts or statements. 'That' as an idea.

items.question: _____ día fue perfecto.
items.translation: (That day was perfect)
items.explanation: 'Ese día' for a specific past day. Medium distance demonstrative for recent past.

items.question: Todo _____ es verdad.
items.translation: (All that is true)
items.explanation: Neuter 'eso' for 'that' referring to whole statements or abstract ideas.

items.question: _____ mesas son nuevas.
items.translation: (Those tables are new - near listener)
items.explanation: 'Esas' (feminine plural) for things near listener. Agrees with 'mesas'.

items.question: _____ tiempos eran mejores.
items.translation: (Those times were better - long ago)
items.explanation: 'Aquellos' for distant past. Nostalgic references use 'aquel' forms.

name: Possessive Adjectives & Pronouns
description: Learn mi, tu, su and long forms

items.question: _____ casa es grande.
items.translation: (My house is big)
items.explanation: Short form 'mi' (my) before singular noun. Doesn't change for gender.

items.question: _____ hermanos viven en Madrid.
items.translation: (My brothers live in Madrid)
items.explanation: 'Mis' (plural) before plural nouns. Short possessive agrees only in number.

items.question: Esta casa es _____.
items.translation: (This house is mine)
items.explanation: Long form 'mía' after 'ser'. Agrees in gender with thing possessed (casa = feminine).

items.question: _____ padre es médico.
items.translation: (Your father is a doctor - informal)
items.explanation: Short form 'tu' (your informal) before noun. No accent, unlike pronoun 'tú'.

items.question: _____ coche es nuevo.
items.translation: (His/her car is new)
items.explanation: 'Su/sus' means his/her/your formal/their. Context clarifies whose.

items.question: Los libros son _____.
items.translation: (The books are ours)
items.explanation: Long form 'nuestros' after 'ser'. Agrees with masculine plural 'libros'.

items.question: Una amiga _____ vino ayer.
items.translation: (A friend of mine came yesterday)
items.explanation: Long form after noun: 'amiga mía'. Means 'a friend of mine'.

items.question: ¿Es _____ esta chaqueta?
items.translation: (Is this jacket yours? - informal)
items.explanation: Long form 'tuya' after 'ser'. Feminine agrees with 'chaqueta'.

items.question: _____ padres están aquí.
items.translation: (Our parents are here)
items.explanation: Short form 'nuestros' before plural masculine noun. Agrees in gender and number.

items.question: Ese perro es _____.
items.translation: (That dog is theirs)
items.explanation: Long form 'suyo' after 'ser'. Masculine agrees with 'perro'.

items.question: _____ ideas son buenas.
items.translation: (Your ideas are good - formal/their)
items.explanation: 'Sus' (plural) before plural nouns. Can mean your (formal) or their.

items.question: La casa es _____.
items.translation: (The house is yours - formal)
items.explanation: Long form 'suya' for formal 'yours'. Feminine agrees with 'casa'.

items.question: _____ hermana es doctora.
items.translation: (Your sister is a doctor - informal)
items.explanation: Short form 'tu' before singular noun. Different from pronoun 'tú' (you).

items.question: Estos son _____.
items.translation: (These are yours - informal)
items.explanation: Long form 'tuyos' after 'ser'. Masculine plural for unspecified masculine items.

items.question: _____ familia es grande.
items.translation: (Our family is big)
items.explanation: 'Nuestra' (feminine singular) before feminine singular noun. 'Nuestro/a' agrees in gender.

name: Conjunctions and Connectors
description: Link ideas with y, pero, porque and more

items.question: Juan _____ María son hermanos.
items.translation: (Juan and María are siblings)
items.explanation: 'Y' means 'and' for connecting words. Standard conjunction.

items.question: Padre _____ hijo vinieron.
items.translation: (Father and son came)
items.explanation: 'Y' becomes 'e' before words starting with 'i' or 'hi'. Pronunciation rule.

items.question: Es difícil _____ importante.
items.translation: (It's difficult but important)
items.explanation: 'Pero' means 'but' for contrasting ideas. Most common adversative conjunction.

items.question: No es rojo _____ azul.
items.translation: (It's not red but blue)
items.explanation: 'Sino' means 'but rather' after negatives. Use when contradicting negative statement.

items.question: ¿Café _____ té?
items.translation: (Coffee or tea?)
items.explanation: 'O' means 'or' for alternatives. Standard disjunctive conjunction.

items.question: Mujer _____ hombre puede aplicar.
items.translation: (Woman or man can apply)
items.explanation: 'O' becomes 'u' before words starting with 'o' or 'ho'. Pronunciation rule.

items.question: Estoy cansado _____ trabajé mucho.
items.translation: (I'm tired because I worked a lot)
items.explanation: 'Porque' (one word) means 'because' in statements. Explains reason.

items.question: ¿_____ estás triste?
items.translation: (Why are you sad?)
items.explanation: 'Por qué' (two words) means 'why' in questions. Has written accent.

items.question: Es inteligente _____ no estudia.
items.translation: (He's intelligent but he doesn't study)
items.explanation: 'Pero' contrasts two clauses. Both clauses can be affirmative or negative.

items.question: _____ llueva, iremos.
items.translation: (Although it rains, we'll go)
items.explanation: 'Aunque' means 'although/even though'. Introduces concessive clause.

items.question: Estudia español _____ francés.
items.translation: (She studies Spanish and French)
items.explanation: 'Y' before consonants and most words. Standard 'and'.

items.question: No solo habla español _____ también portugués.
items.translation: (He speaks not only Spanish but also Portuguese)
items.explanation: 'No solo...sino también' means 'not only...but also'. 'Sino' after negative.

items.question: _____ tengo hambre, comeré después.
items.translation: (If I'm hungry, I'll eat later)
items.explanation: 'Si' (without accent) means 'if'. Different from 'sí' (yes).

items.question: Llegaré _____ pueda.
items.translation: (I'll arrive when I can)
items.explanation: 'Cuando' (without accent) means 'when' in statements. Different from question '¿cuándo?'.

items.question: Es tarde, _____ me voy.
items.translation: (It's late, so I'm leaving)
items.explanation: 'Así que' means 'so/therefore'. Shows consequence or result.

name: Time Expressions
description: Master telling time and time-related phrases

items.question: Son _____ tres.
items.translation: (It's three o'clock)
items.explanation: Use feminine plural article 'las' for hours. 'Las tres' (three o'clock).

items.question: Es _____ una.
items.translation: (It's one o'clock)
items.explanation: One o'clock uses singular 'la una'. All other hours use plural 'las'.

items.question: Son las dos _____ media.
items.translation: (It's two thirty / half past two)
items.explanation: 'Y media' means 'and a half'. Standard for 30 minutes past.

items.question: Son las cinco _____ cuarto.
items.translation: (It's quarter to five)
items.explanation: 'Menos cuarto' means 'minus quarter'. Use 'menos' for minutes before the hour.

items.question: _____ qué hora es la clase?
items.translation: (At what time is the class?)
items.explanation: 'A qué hora' asks 'at what time'. Use 'a' for time references.

items.question: La reunión es _____ las diez.
items.translation: (The meeting is at ten)
items.explanation: 'A' before time means 'at'. 'A las diez' = at ten o'clock.

items.question: Trabajo _____ la mañana.
items.translation: (I work in the morning)
items.explanation: 'Por la mañana/tarde/noche' means 'in the morning/afternoon/night'. Standard pattern.

items.question: Llegó _____ medianoche.
items.translation: (He arrived at midnight)
items.explanation: 'A medianoche' means 'at midnight'. Use 'a' for specific times.

items.question: _____ viene los lunes.
items.translation: (He comes on Mondays)
items.explanation: No preposition needed for repeated days. 'Los lunes' alone means 'on Mondays'.

items.question: Nos vemos _____ una semana.
items.translation: (We'll see each other in a week)
items.explanation: 'En' for future time expressions. 'En una semana' = in a week.

items.question: Hace dos años _____ vivo aquí.
items.translation: (I've been living here for two years)
items.explanation: 'Hace + time + que' expresses duration. 'Hace dos años que' = for two years.

items.question: _____ el lunes tengo examen.
items.translation: (On Monday I have an exam)
items.explanation: Use article 'el' for single occurrence on specific day. 'El lunes' = on Monday.

items.question: Regreso _____ dos días.
items.translation: (I return in two days)
items.explanation: 'En' for future time frame. 'En dos días' means within/in two days.

items.question: Estudio _____ las noches.
items.translation: (I study at night)
items.explanation: 'Por las noches' means 'at night' (habitually). Use 'por' for time periods.

items.question: Salgo _____ mediodía.
items.translation: (I leave at noon)
items.explanation: 'Al mediodía' uses contraction. 'A + el = al' for noon.

name: Location and Direction
description: Express where things are and movement

items.question: El libro está _____ la mesa.
items.translation: (The book is on the table)
items.explanation: 'Sobre' means 'on/on top of'. 'En' also works; 'sobre' is more specific.

items.question: Vivo _____ Madrid.
items.translation: (I live in Madrid)
items.explanation: 'En' for being in a city or place. 'Vivir en' is standard.

items.question: Voy _____ Madrid.
items.translation: (I'm going to Madrid)
items.explanation: 'A' indicates motion toward. 'Ir a' (go to) is the standard pattern.

items.question: El gato está _____ la cama.
items.translation: (The cat is under the bed)
items.explanation: 'Debajo de' means 'under'. Compound preposition requires 'de'.

items.question: La tienda está _____ la esquina.
items.translation: (The store is on the corner)
items.explanation: 'En la esquina' means 'on the corner'. Standard location expression.

items.question: Camino _____ la escuela.
items.translation: (I walk toward school)
items.explanation: 'Hacia' means 'toward' (direction). Different from 'a' which implies arrival.

items.question: Está _____ mi casa.
items.translation: (It's behind my house)
items.explanation: 'Detrás de' means 'behind'. Opposite of 'delante de' (in front of).

items.question: El museo está _____ aquí.
items.translation: (The museum is near here)
items.explanation: 'Cerca de' means 'near/close to'. Always requires 'de'.

items.question: Pon el vaso _____ la mesa.
items.translation: (Put the glass on the table)
items.explanation: 'En' for placing things on surfaces. 'Poner en' is standard.

items.question: Vienen _____ Argentina.
items.translation: (They come from Argentina)
items.explanation: 'De' shows origin. 'Venir/ser de' indicates where you're from.

items.question: El perro está _____ la casa.
items.translation: (The dog is inside the house)
items.explanation: 'Dentro de' means 'inside/within'. Opposite of 'fuera de' (outside).

items.question: Corro _____ el parque.
items.translation: (I run to the park)
items.explanation: 'Hasta' means 'until/up to' indicating endpoint. 'Correr hasta' = run to (the point).

items.question: Está _____ la puerta.
items.translation: (It's in front of the door)
items.explanation: 'Delante de' means 'in front of'. Opposite of 'detrás de'.

items.question: Vive _____ de la ciudad.
items.translation: (She lives outside the city)
items.explanation: 'Fuera de' means 'outside'. Opposite of 'dentro de' (inside).

items.question: El banco está _____ la farmacia.
items.translation: (The bank is next to the pharmacy)
items.explanation: 'Al lado de' means 'next to/beside'. Common location expression.

name: Personal 'A'
description: Learn when to use 'a' before people

items.question: Veo _____ María.
items.translation: (I see María)
items.explanation: Use 'a' before specific people as direct objects. 'Ver a María' requires personal 'a'.

items.question: Busco _____ mi amigo.
items.translation: (I'm looking for my friend)
items.explanation: Personal 'a' with 'buscar' before people. 'Buscar a' someone requires it.

items.question: Tengo dos hermanos _____.
items.translation: (I have two brothers)
items.explanation: No personal 'a' with 'tener' (have). Exception to the rule.

items.question: Conozco _____ tu padre.
items.translation: (I know your father)
items.explanation: 'Conocer' (know person) requires personal 'a'. 'Conocer a' before people.

items.question: Veo _____ perro en el parque.
items.translation: (I see a dog in the park)
items.explanation: No personal 'a' for animals unless they're pets with names. Generic dog = no 'a'.

items.question: Espero _____ Juan.
items.translation: (I'm waiting for Juan)
items.explanation: 'Esperar a' (wait for) someone uses personal 'a'. Required before specific people.

items.question: Necesitamos _____ un profesor.
items.translation: (We need a teacher)
items.explanation: Indefinite 'un profesor' (any teacher) doesn't take personal 'a'. Only specific people do.

items.question: Escucho _____ mi madre.
items.translation: (I listen to my mother)
items.explanation: 'Escuchar a' someone uses personal 'a'. Required before specific people.

items.question: Quiero mucho _____ mis padres.
items.translation: (I love my parents very much)
items.explanation: 'Querer a' (love) people requires personal 'a'. Always needed with people.

items.question: Vi _____ alguien en la calle.
items.translation: (I saw someone in the street)
items.explanation: Personal 'a' with 'alguien' (someone). Even indefinite people can take it.

items.question: No conozco _____ nadie aquí.
items.translation: (I don't know anyone here)
items.explanation: 'Nadie' (no one) as person takes personal 'a'. 'Conocer a nadie'.

items.question: Hay _____ personas en la sala.
items.translation: (There are people in the room)
items.explanation: 'Hay' never uses personal 'a'. Exception even with people.

items.question: Invité _____ mis amigos.
items.translation: (I invited my friends)
items.explanation: 'Invitar a' (invite) uses personal 'a' before people. Standard pattern.

items.question: Visito _____ mi abuela.
items.translation: (I visit my grandmother)
items.explanation: 'Visitar a' someone uses personal 'a'. Required for visiting people.

items.question: Veo _____ mi perro Firulais.
items.translation: (I see my dog Firulais)
items.explanation: Named pets get personal 'a'. 'Firulais' is specific, so use 'a'.

items.question: Un café_____.
items.translation: (A little coffee)
items.explanation: Words ending in consonants add -cito/a. 'Café' + 'cito' = 'cafecito'.

items.question: Una casa_____.
items.translation: (A little house)
items.explanation: Words ending in -a drop it and add -ita. 'Casa' becomes 'casita'.

items.question: Un perro_____.
items.translation: (A big dog)
items.explanation: Augmentative -ón indicates bigger size. 'Perro' + 'ón' = 'perrón'.

items.question: Mi abuel_____.
items.translation: (My dear grandmother / granny)
items.explanation: Drop final vowel, add -ita for affection. 'Abuela' becomes 'abuelita'.

items.question: Un libr_____.
items.translation: (A huge book)
items.explanation: Augmentative -ote indicates large size. 'Libro' + 'ote' = 'librote'.

items.question: Una flor_____.
items.translation: (A little flower)
items.explanation: Words ending in consonant add -cita. 'Flor' + 'cita' = 'florcita'.

items.question: Un moment_____.
items.translation: (Just a little moment)
items.explanation: Drop final vowel (if present), add -ito. 'Momento' becomes 'momentito'.

items.question: Una sill_____.
items.translation: (A small chair)
items.explanation: Regional variation: -ita is most common diminutive. 'Silla' becomes 'sillita'.

items.question: Un hombr_____.
items.translation: (A big man)
items.explanation: Augmentative -ón for large people. 'Hombre' becomes 'hombrón'.

items.question: Una manecilla_____.
items.translation: (A tiny hand / little hand)
items.explanation: 'Manecilla' already contains diminutive -illa. Already means 'little hand'.

items.question: Un beb_____.
items.translation: (A tiny baby)
items.explanation: Drop accent and final vowel, add -ito. 'Bebé' becomes 'bebito'.

items.question: Un golp_____.
items.translation: (A big blow / strong hit)
items.explanation: Augmentative -azo can intensify impact. 'Golpe' + 'azo' = 'golpazo' (big hit).

items.question: Mi herman_____.
items.translation: (My little brother)
items.explanation: Common diminutive for family. 'Hermano' becomes 'hermanito' (little brother).

items.question: Una cuchar_____.
items.translation: (A little spoon / teaspoon)
items.explanation: Drop -a, add -ita. 'Cuchara' becomes 'cucharita' (little spoon).

items.question: Un coch_____.
items.translation: (A big car)
items.explanation: Augmentative -azo emphasizes size. 'Coche' + 'azo' = 'cochazo' (big/fancy car).

name: Verb Periphrases
description: Master ir a, tener que, acabar de patterns

items.question: _____ _____ estudiar mañana.
items.translation: (I'm going to study tomorrow)
items.explanation: 'Ir a + infinitive' expresses future plans. Most common future expression in speech.

items.question: _____ _____ terminar este trabajo.
items.translation: (I have to finish this work)
items.explanation: 'Tener que + infinitive' expresses obligation. Means 'have to/must'.

items.question: _____ _____ llegar.
items.translation: (I just arrived)
items.explanation: 'Acabar de + infinitive' means 'just did'. Expresses recent past action.

items.question: _____ _____ llover.
items.translation: (It's starting to rain)
items.explanation: 'Empezar a + infinitive' means 'start to'. Beginning of action.

items.question: _____ _____ trabajar a las 8.
items.translation: (I usually work at 8)
items.explanation: 'Soler + infinitive' expresses habitual action. Means 'usually do'.

items.question: _____ _____ salir.
items.translation: (We're about to leave)
items.explanation: 'Estar por + infinitive' means 'about to'. Imminent action.

items.question: _____ _____ leer más.
items.translation: (I should read more)
items.explanation: 'Deber + infinitive' expresses should/ought. Conditional 'debería' is softer.

items.question: _____ _____ estudiar cuando llamaste.
items.translation: (I was about to study when you called)
items.explanation: 'Ir a' in imperfect expresses 'was going to'. Interrupted future in past.

items.question: _____ _____ comer.
items.translation: (I just finished eating)
items.explanation: 'Acabar de' means 'just finished'. Recent completion.

items.question: _____ _____ fumar hace un año.
items.translation: (I stopped smoking a year ago)
items.explanation: 'Dejar de + infinitive' means 'stop doing'. Cessation of action.

items.question: _____ _____ ayudarte.
items.translation: (I can help you)
items.explanation: 'Poder + infinitive' expresses ability/permission. Means 'can/able to'.

items.question: _____ _____ aprender español hace dos años.
items.translation: (I started learning Spanish two years ago)
items.explanation: 'Empezar a + infinitive' marks beginning. Use preterite for specific start time.

items.question: No _____ _____ hacerlo.
items.translation: (I don't dare to do it)
items.explanation: 'Atreverse a + infinitive' means 'dare to'. Reflexive verb + a.

items.question: _____ _____ practicar más.
items.translation: (You need to practice more)
items.explanation: 'Necesitar + infinitive' expresses need. Similar to 'tener que' but slightly softer.

items.question: Está _____ _____ llover.
items.translation: (It's about to rain)
items.explanation: 'Estar a punto de + infinitive' means 'about to'. Imminent action expression.

items.question: No veo a _____.
items.translation: (I don't see anyone)
items.explanation: Spanish requires double negative after 'no'. Use 'nadie' (nobody) after negative verb.

items.question: _____ viene nadie.
items.translation: (Nobody is coming)
items.explanation: Without 'no' before verb, 'nadie' can start sentence. With verb first, use 'no' + 'nadie'.

items.question: No tengo _____ que decir.
items.translation: (I have nothing to say)
items.explanation: After negative 'no tengo', use 'nada' (nothing). Double negative is required.

items.question: _____ he estado en París.
items.translation: (I have never been to Paris)
items.explanation: 'Nunca' at sentence start doesn't need 'no'. If after verb: 'No he estado nunca'.

items.question: No compré _____ libro.
items.translation: (I didn't buy any book)
items.explanation: After negative verb, use 'ningún' (no/not any) before masculine singular nouns.

items.question: _____ lo he visto _____.
items.translation: (I have never seen it)
items.explanation: 'No' before verb + 'nunca' after creates double negative. Both are required.

items.question: No conoce a _____ aquí.
items.translation: (He doesn't know anyone here)
items.explanation: After negative 'no conoce', use 'nadie' for 'anyone'. Double negative pattern.

items.question: _____ tampoco sabe.
items.translation: (She doesn't know either)
items.explanation: 'Tampoco' (neither/either) already contains negative meaning. No 'no' needed before verb.

items.question: Ni tú _____ yo vamos.
items.translation: (Neither you nor I are going)
items.explanation: 'Ni...ni' means 'neither...nor'. Creates double negative without 'no'.

items.question: No hay _____ problema.
items.translation: (There is no problem)
items.explanation: After negative 'no hay', use 'ningún' before masculine nouns.

items.question: _____ tengo tiempo.
items.translation: (I never have time)
items.explanation: 'Nunca' before verb eliminates need for 'no'. Alternative: 'No tengo nunca tiempo'.

items.question: No quiero _____ café _____ té.
items.translation: (I don't want either coffee or tea)
items.explanation: After negative, 'ni...ni' replaces 'o...o'. Means 'neither...nor'.

items.question: _____ he comido _____.
items.translation: (I haven't eaten anything)
items.explanation: 'No' before verb + 'nada' after. Both negatives required in Spanish.

items.question: _____ estudiante vino a clase.
items.translation: (No student came to class)
items.explanation: 'Ningún' before noun at sentence start doesn't need 'no'. Already negative.

items.question: No voy _____.
items.translation: (I'm not going anywhere)
items.explanation: 'A ninguna parte' means 'nowhere/to no place'. After 'no', use negative form.

items.question: _____ comiendo ahora.
items.translation: (I am eating now)
items.explanation: Progressive uses 'estar' + gerund. 'Estoy comiendo' for action in progress.

items.question: Ella está _____.
items.translation: (She is studying)
items.explanation: Gerund for -ar verbs: remove -ar, add -ando. 'Estudiar' becomes 'estudiando'.

items.question: _____ viendo la tele.
items.translation: (We are watching TV)
items.explanation: First person plural 'estamos' + gerund. Progressive shows ongoing action.

items.question: Están _____.
items.translation: (They are writing)
items.explanation: Gerund for -ir verbs: remove -ir, add -iendo. 'Escribir' becomes 'escribiendo'.

items.question: ¿Qué estás _____?
items.translation: (What are you doing?)
items.explanation: 'Hacer' has irregular gerund 'haciendo'. Some verbs have stem changes in gerund.

items.question: _____ durmiendo.
items.translation: (He is sleeping)
items.explanation: Third person singular 'está' + gerund for he/she is doing.

items.question: Estoy _____ un libro.
items.translation: (I am reading a book)
items.explanation: 'Leer' has irregular gerund 'leyendo'. -Er verbs with vowel stem change y.

items.question: _____ lloviendo afuera.
items.translation: (It is raining outside)
items.explanation: Weather in progress uses 'está' + gerund. 'Está lloviendo' for current rain.

items.question: Estaban _____ cuando llegué.
items.translation: (They were talking when I arrived)
items.explanation: Imperfect 'estaban' + gerund for past progressive. Shows ongoing past action.

items.question: ¿Estás _____ español?
items.translation: (Are you learning Spanish?)
items.explanation: Present progressive 'estás aprendiendo' for current learning process.

items.question: No estoy _____.
items.translation: (I'm not listening)
items.explanation: Negative progressive: 'no estoy' + gerund. Negates ongoing action.

items.question: Ella está _____ la cena.
items.translation: (She is preparing dinner)
items.explanation: Regular -ar verb gerund: 'preparar' becomes 'preparando'.

items.question: Estoy _____ de ti.
items.translation: (I am thinking of you)
items.explanation: Stem-changing verbs keep stem in gerund usually. 'Pensar' becomes 'pensando'.

items.question: _____ corriendo en el parque.
items.translation: (They are running in the park)
items.explanation: 'Están' (third plural) + gerund for they are doing.

items.question: Ella está _____ música.
items.translation: (She is listening to music)
items.explanation: 'Oír' has irregular gerund 'oyendo'. Similar to 'leer' → 'leyendo'.

items.question: Yo _____ estudiante.
items.translation: (I am a student)
items.explanation: 'Ser' is completely irregular. First person 'soy' doesn't follow patterns.

items.question: _____ al cine mañana.
items.translation: (I'm going to the movies tomorrow)
items.explanation: 'Ir' is irregular. First person present is 'voy', not following -ir rules.

items.question: _____ una casa grande.
items.translation: (I have a big house)
items.explanation: 'Tener' is irregular with -go ending in first person. 'Tengo' pattern.

items.question: Yo _____ la verdad.
items.translation: (I know the truth)
items.explanation: 'Saber' has irregular first person 'sé'. Completely irregular form.

items.question: _____ mi tarea todos los días.
items.translation: (I do my homework every day)
items.explanation: 'Hacer' has -go ending in first person. 'Hago' is irregular.

items.question: Yo _____ de mi casa a las 8.
items.translation: (I leave my house at 8)
items.explanation: 'Salir' adds -go in first person present. Common irregular pattern.

items.question: _____ la comida en la mesa.
items.translation: (I put the food on the table)
items.explanation: 'Poner' has -go irregular ending. 'Pongo' for first person.

items.question: Yo _____ a tu hermana.
items.translation: (I know your sister)
items.explanation: 'Conocer' adds -zco in first person. Pattern for -cer/-cir verbs.

items.question: _____ mucha agua.
items.translation: (I drink a lot of water)
items.explanation: 'Beber' is regular despite being common. Follow normal -er patterns.

items.question: Yo _____ aquí los lunes.
items.translation: (I come here on Mondays)
items.explanation: 'Venir' has -go ending and stem change. 'Vengo' is first person.

items.question: _____ un regalo para ti.
items.translation: (I'm bringing a gift for you)
items.explanation: 'Traer' has irregular -igo ending. 'Traigo' for first person.

items.question: Yo _____ temprano.
items.translation: (I fall asleep early)
items.explanation: 'Dormir' has stem change o→ue. Reflexive 'dormirse' = 'me duermo'.

items.question: _____ español e inglés.
items.translation: (I speak Spanish and English)
items.explanation: 'Hablar' is actually regular -ar verb. Not all common verbs are irregular.

items.question: Yo _____ ejercicio.
items.translation: (I do exercise)
items.explanation: 'Hacer ejercicio' uses irregular 'hago'. Fixed expression for exercise.

items.question: _____ muchos amigos.
items.translation: (I have many friends)
items.explanation: 'Tener' irregular -go ending repeats here. 'Tengo amigos'.

items.question: _____ dos años _____ vivo aquí.
items.translation: (I have been living here for two years)
items.explanation: 'Hace + time + que' expresses duration. Formula for 'for' with time periods.

items.question: Vivo aquí _____ hace dos años.
items.translation: (I have been living here for two years)
items.explanation: Alternative: 'desde hace' at end of sentence. Both structures express duration.

items.question: ¿_____ tiempo estudias español?
items.translation: (How long have you been studying Spanish?)
items.explanation: '¿Cuánto tiempo?' asks 'how long'. Question about duration.

items.question: Hace una hora _____ llegó.
items.translation: (He arrived an hour ago)
items.explanation: 'Hace + time + que' for 'ago' with preterite. Different from duration meaning.

items.question: _____ tres meses que trabajo aquí.
items.translation: (I have been working here for three months)
items.explanation: 'Hace' at start + present tense shows ongoing duration. Still working.

items.question: ¿Desde _____ vives en Madrid?
items.translation: (Since when do you live in Madrid?)
items.explanation: '¿Desde cuándo?' asks 'since when'. Question about starting point.

items.question: Hace mucho tiempo _____ no la veo.
items.translation: (I haven't seen her in a long time)
items.explanation: 'Hace + time + que + no' for negative duration. How long not doing.

items.question: Estudio español _____ seis meses.
items.translation: (I have been studying Spanish for six months)
items.explanation: 'Desde hace' at sentence end. Alternative to 'hace...que' at start.

items.question: _____ un año que se casaron.
items.translation: (They got married a year ago)
items.explanation: 'Hace' + preterite for 'ago'. Completed action in past.

items.question: ¿_____ cuánto tiempo esperas?
items.translation: (How long have you been waiting?)
items.explanation: '¿Desde cuánto tiempo?' alternative to '¿Cuánto tiempo hace que?'. Both ask duration.

items.question: Hace dos días _____ comí pizza.
items.translation: (I ate pizza two days ago)
items.explanation: 'Hace + time + que + preterite' for 'ago'. Specific past action.

items.question: _____ mucho que no nos vemos.
items.translation: (We haven't seen each other in a long time)
items.explanation: 'Hace mucho que no' for long time not doing. Negative duration.

items.question: Trabajo aquí _____ 2020.
items.translation: (I have been working here since 2020)
items.explanation: 'Desde' + specific date/year for 'since'. Starting point in time.

items.question: _____ poco que empezó a llover.
items.translation: (It started raining a short while ago)
items.explanation: 'Hace poco que' for 'recently/short while ago'. Recent past.

items.question: ¿Cuánto tiempo _____ que estudias?
items.translation: (How long have you been studying?)
items.explanation: '¿Cuánto tiempo hace que?' standard question for duration. Most common form.

items.question: Yo _____ estudiar más.
items.translation: (I want to study more)
items.explanation: 'Querer' changes e→ie in stressed syllables. First person 'quiero'.

items.question: ¿_____ ir al cine?
items.translation: (Do you want to go to the movies? - informal)
items.explanation: Stem change e→ie in second person. 'Quieres' for tú form.

items.question: Ella _____ bien español.
items.translation: (She understands Spanish well)
items.explanation: 'Entender' changes e→ie. Third person 'entiende' with stem change.

items.question: Nosotros no _____ la puerta.
items.translation: (We don't close the door)
items.explanation: 'Cerrar' has e→ie change, but NOT in nosotros/vosotros. 'Cerramos' is regular.

items.question: ¿A qué hora _____ la tienda?
items.translation: (What time does the store close?)
items.explanation: 'Cerrar' changes e→ie in third person. 'Cierra' with stem change.

items.question: Yo _____ a las 8.
items.translation: (I sleep at 8)
items.explanation: 'Dormir' changes o→ue in stressed syllables. First person 'duermo'.

items.question: Ellos _____ en un hotel.
items.translation: (They sleep in a hotel)
items.explanation: 'Dormir' changes o→ue in third plural. 'Duermen' with stem change.

items.question: Yo _____ café todas las mañanas.
items.translation: (I order coffee every morning)
items.explanation: 'Pedir' changes e→i (only -ir verbs). First person 'pido'.

items.question: Nosotros _____ aquí.
items.translation: (We sleep here)
items.explanation: No stem change in nosotros/vosotros forms. 'Dormimos' is regular.

items.question: Él _____ ayuda.
items.translation: (He asks for help)
items.explanation: 'Pedir' e→i change in third person. 'Pide' with stem change.

items.question: ¿_____ tú el número?
items.translation: (Do you repeat the number?)
items.explanation: 'Repetir' changes e→i. Second person 'repites' with change.

items.question: Nosotros _____ la lección.
items.translation: (We repeat the lesson)
items.explanation: No stem change in nosotros. 'Repetimos' stays regular.

items.question: Yo _____ la ventana.
items.translation: (I close the window)
items.explanation: 'Cerrar' changes e→ie. First person 'cierro' with stem change.

items.question: Ellos _____ el libro.
items.translation: (They find the book)
items.explanation: 'Encontrar' changes o→ue. Third plural 'encuentran' with change.

items.question: ¿_____ tú almorzar conmigo?
items.translation: (Do you want to have lunch with me?)
items.explanation: 'Querer' e→ie in second person. 'Quieres' for tú.

items.question: _____ encanta la música.
items.translation: (I love music)
items.explanation: 'Encantar' (love/delight) follows gustar pattern. 'Me encanta' = it delights me.

items.question: _____ molestan los mosquitos.
items.translation: (Mosquitos bother you - informal)
items.explanation: 'Molestar' works like gustar. Plural 'molestan' because 'mosquitos' is plural.

items.question: _____ parece buena idea.
items.translation: (It seems like a good idea to me)
items.explanation: 'Parecer' (seem) follows gustar pattern. 'Me parece' = it seems to me.

items.question: _____ importa tu opinión.
items.translation: (Your opinion matters to us)
items.explanation: 'Importar' (matter) uses gustar structure. 'Nos importa' = it matters to us.

items.question: _____ sobra dinero.
items.translation: (I have money left over)
items.explanation: 'Sobrar' (be left over) follows gustar. 'Me sobra' = there's excess for me.

items.question: _____ caen bien tus amigos.
items.translation: (I like your friends - they fall well to me)
items.explanation: 'Caer bien' (like person) uses gustar pattern. Plural 'caen' for 'amigos'.

items.question: _____ hacen falta más sillas.
items.translation: (We need more chairs)
items.explanation: 'Hacer falta' (need) follows gustar. Plural 'hacen' for plural 'sillas'.

items.question: _____ toca pagar la cuenta.
items.translation: (It's your turn to pay the check)
items.explanation: 'Tocar' (be one's turn) uses gustar structure. 'Te toca' = it's your turn.

items.question: _____ conviene descansar.
items.translation: (It's advisable for him to rest)
items.explanation: 'Convenir' (be advisable) follows gustar. 'Le conviene' = it suits him.

items.question: _____ apetece un helado.
items.translation: (I feel like ice cream)
items.explanation: 'Apetecer' (feel like) uses gustar pattern. 'Me apetece' = it appeals to me.

items.question: _____ cuesta entender esto.
items.translation: (It's hard for me to understand this)
items.explanation: 'Costar' (be difficult) follows gustar. 'Me cuesta' = it's difficult for me.

items.question: _____ da igual.
items.translation: (It's all the same to me / I don't care)
items.explanation: 'Dar igual' (not matter) uses gustar structure. 'Me da igual' = I don't care.

items.question: _____ alegra verte.
items.translation: (I'm happy to see you)
items.explanation: 'Alegrar' (make happy) follows gustar. 'Me alegra' = it makes me happy.

items.question: _____ sorprenden tus comentarios.
items.translation: (Your comments surprise them)
items.explanation: 'Sorprender' uses gustar pattern. Plural 'sorprenden' for plural 'comentarios'.

items.question: _____ bastan dos euros.
items.translation: (Two euros are enough for me)
items.explanation: 'Bastar' (be enough) follows gustar. Plural 'bastan' for 'euros'.

name: Question Words
description: Master qué, cuál, quién, dónde, etc.

items.question: ¿_____ te llamas?
items.translation: (What is your name?)
items.explanation: '¿Cómo?' for names and manner. '¿Cómo te llamas?' is standard greeting.

items.question: ¿_____ años tienes?
items.translation: (How old are you?)
items.explanation: '¿Cuántos?' for plural countable things. 'Años' is plural masculine.

items.question: ¿_____ vives?
items.translation: (Where do you live?)
items.explanation: '¿Dónde?' (with accent) for questions about location. 'Donde' (no accent) for statements.

items.question: ¿_____ es tu cumpleaños?
items.translation: (When is your birthday?)
items.explanation: '¿Cuándo?' (with accent) for time questions. 'Cuando' (no accent) means 'when' in statements.

items.question: ¿_____ es esto?
items.translation: (What is this?)
items.explanation: '¿Qué?' for definitions or identification. Asking for explanation.

items.question: ¿_____ estudias español?
items.translation: (Why do you study Spanish?)
items.explanation: '¿Por qué?' (two words) means 'why'. 'Porque' (one word) means 'because'.

items.question: ¿_____ es tu profesor?
items.translation: (Who is your teacher?)
items.explanation: '¿Quién?' (with accent) asks 'who'. For people.

items.question: ¿_____ prefieres, café o té?
items.translation: (Which do you prefer, coffee or tea?)
items.explanation: '¿Cuál?' for choosing between options. Asking for selection.

items.question: ¿_____ cuesta este libro?
items.translation: (How much does this book cost?)
items.explanation: '¿Cuánto?' for quantity or price. Singular for uncountable amounts.

items.question: ¿_____ libro quieres?
items.translation: (Which book do you want?)
items.explanation: '¿Qué?' before noun means 'which/what'. '¿Qué libro?' = which book.

items.question: ¿De _____ eres?
items.translation: (Where are you from?)
items.explanation: '¿De dónde?' asks about origin. Standard question for nationality.

items.question: ¿_____ son estos?
items.translation: (Whose are these?)
items.explanation: '¿De quién?' asks about possession. 'Whose' in questions.

items.question: ¿_____ vas?
items.translation: (Where are you going?)
items.explanation: '¿Adónde?' with motion verbs. 'To where' vs just 'where'.

items.question: ¿_____ lo haces?
items.translation: (How do you do it?)
items.explanation: '¿Cómo?' asks about manner or method. How something is done.

items.question: ¿_____ estudias?
items.translation: (How much do you study?)
items.explanation: '¿Cuánto?' for extent or degree. How much (of something).

items.question: Yo _____ a tu hermana.
items.translation: (I know your sister)
items.explanation: 'Conocer' for knowing people. Use with 'a' before people.

items.question: _____ dónde vive ella.
items.translation: (I know where she lives)
items.explanation: 'Saber' for knowing facts or information. Use for what/where/when knowledge.

items.question: ¿_____ Madrid?
items.translation: (Do you know Madrid? - are you familiar)
items.explanation: 'Conocer' for familiarity with places. Being acquainted with cities.

items.question: _____ hablar español.
items.translation: (I know how to speak Spanish)
items.explanation: 'Saber' + infinitive for skills/how to. 'Sé hablar' = I know how to speak.

items.question: No _____ a nadie aquí.
items.translation: (I don't know anyone here)
items.explanation: 'Conocer' for knowing/being acquainted with people. 'Conocer a nadie'.

items.question: _____ la respuesta.
items.translation: (I know the answer)
items.explanation: 'Saber' for knowing facts or answers. Information knowledge.

items.question: ¿_____ esta canción?
items.translation: (Do you know this song? - familiar with)
items.explanation: 'Conocer' for familiarity with songs, books, movies. Being acquainted.

items.question: _____ que mañana es fiesta.
items.translation: (I know that tomorrow is a holiday)
items.explanation: 'Saber' + que for knowing facts. Factual knowledge.

items.question: ¿_____ cocinar?
items.translation: (Do you know how to cook?)
items.explanation: 'Saber' + infinitive for abilities. Knowing how to do something.

items.question: _____ un buen restaurante.
items.translation: (I know a good restaurant)
items.explanation: 'Conocer' for being familiar with places or establishments.

items.question: No _____ su número de teléfono.
items.translation: (I don't know his phone number)
items.explanation: 'Saber' for specific information like numbers, addresses.

items.question: Quiero _____ más sobre la cultura.
items.translation: (I want to know more about the culture)
items.explanation: 'Saber' for learning information. 'Conocer' would imply direct experience.

items.question: _____ bien esta ciudad.
items.translation: (I know this city well)
items.explanation: 'Conocer' for familiarity with places. Being well acquainted.

items.question: _____ tocar la guitarra.
items.translation: (He knows how to play guitar)
items.explanation: 'Saber' + infinitive for skills. Musical ability is 'saber tocar'.

items.question: ¿_____ al nuevo profesor?
items.translation: (Do you know the new teacher?)
items.explanation: 'Conocer a' for being acquainted with people. Personal knowledge.

items.question: _____ cincuenta por ciento está de acuerdo.
items.translation: (Fifty percent agrees)
items.explanation: Percentages are masculine. Use 'el' + number + 'por ciento'.

items.question: _____ mitad de los estudiantes aprobó.
items.translation: (Half of the students passed)
items.explanation: 'La mitad' (the half) is feminine. Fixed expression for one half.

items.question: Comí _____ tercio del pastel.
items.translation: (I ate one third of the cake)
items.explanation: Fractions are masculine. 'Un tercio' (one third), 'un cuarto' (one fourth).

items.question: _____ cien por cien de los votos.
items.translation: (One hundred percent of the votes)
items.explanation: 'Por cien' or 'por ciento' both work. Always masculine article.

items.question: Necesito _____ cuarto de kilo.
items.translation: (I need a quarter kilo)
items.explanation: 'Un cuarto' (one quarter) is masculine fraction.

items.question: _____ dos tercios ya están listos.
items.translation: (Two thirds are already ready)
items.explanation: Plural fractions use plural article. 'Los dos tercios' (the two thirds).

items.question: El veinte por ciento _____ falta.
items.translation: (Twenty percent is missing)
items.explanation: Percentages take singular verb. Treated as singular amount.

items.question: Dame _____ media taza de café.
items.translation: (Give me half a cup of coffee)
items.explanation: 'Media/medio' (half) agrees with noun. 'Media taza' (feminine).

items.question: _____ treinta por ciento son mujeres.
items.translation: (Thirty percent are women)
items.explanation: Percentage is masculine even when referring to feminine group.

items.question: Bebí _____ botella.
items.translation: (I drank half the bottle)
items.explanation: 'Media botella' (half bottle). Adjective form agrees with 'botella'.

items.question: _____ quinto de la población vive allí.
items.translation: (One fifth of the population lives there)
items.explanation: Ordinal numbers as fractions are masculine. 'Un quinto' (one fifth).

items.question: El cincuenta por ciento _____ aprobado.
items.translation: (Fifty percent has passed)
items.explanation: Percentage as subject takes singular verb. Singular 'ha'.

items.question: Quedó _____ litro de leche.
items.translation: (Half a liter of milk remained)
items.explanation: 'Medio litro' (masculine). 'Medio/a' agrees with following noun.

items.question: _____ tres cuartos están completos.
items.translation: (Three quarters are complete)
items.explanation: Plural fraction takes plural article. 'Los tres cuartos'.

items.question: Ahorré _____ de mi sueldo.
items.translation: (I saved half of my salary)
items.explanation: 'La mitad de' means 'half of' as noun. Use for general portions.

items.translation: (What's up? - informal greeting)
items.explanation: '¿Qué tal?' is casual 'how are you'. Very common informal greeting.

items.question: Estoy _____.
items.translation: (I'm broke / without money - slang)
items.explanation: 'Estar sin blanca' is slang for broke. Regional informal expression.

items.question: ¡Qué _____!
items.translation: (How cool! / How awesome!)
items.explanation: '¡Qué guay!' is Spanish slang for cool. Regional variations exist.

items.question: Estoy _____.
items.translation: (I'm fed up / sick of it)
items.explanation: 'Estar harto' means fed up. Stronger than just tired.

items.question: _____ un ratito.
items.translation: (Wait a little bit - informal)
items.explanation: 'Espera un ratito' is casual wait. 'Ratito' is diminutive of 'rato'.

items.translation: (Come on! / Let's go!)
items.explanation: '¡Venga!' as interjection means come on. Encouragement in Spain.

items.question: Me da _____ hacerlo.
items.translation: (I'm too lazy to do it - informal)
items.explanation: 'Dar pereza' means to feel lazy about. Informal expression.

items.translation: (Okay / Alright - agreement)
items.explanation: 'Vale' is Spanish for okay/alright. Very common agreement word.

items.question: Tengo un _____ de hambre.
items.translation: (I'm starving - informal)
items.explanation: 'Un montón de' means tons of. Emphasizes large amount informally.

items.question: ¡Qué _____!
items.translation: (What a drag! / How boring!)
items.explanation: '¡Qué rollo!' means what a drag. Colloquial for boring situation.

items.question: No tengo ni _____.
items.translation: (I don't have the slightest idea)
items.explanation: 'No tener ni idea' means no idea at all. Common informal phrase.

items.question: Me _____ de risa.
items.translation: (I died laughing - informal)
items.explanation: 'Morirse de risa' means die laughing. Exaggerated informal expression.

items.question: Estoy _____.
items.translation: (I'm super tired - informal intensifier)
items.explanation: 'Súper' as intensifier is informal. More casual than 'muy'.

items.question: ¡No me _____!
items.translation: (No way! / You're kidding! - disbelief)
items.explanation: '¡No me digas!' expresses surprise/disbelief. Common informal reaction.

items.question: Me da _____ pedirle ayuda.
items.translation: (I'm embarrassed to ask him for help)
items.explanation: 'Dar vergüenza' means to be embarrassed. Common with asking favors.

items.question: ¿Cómo _____ usted?
items.translation: (How are you? - formal)
items.explanation: 'Usted' uses third person conjugation. 'Está' not 'estás' for formal.

items.question: _____ ayudarte.
items.translation: (I can help you - informal)
items.explanation: With 'tú' use any verb form. 'Ayudarte' (not 'ayudarle') shows informal.

items.question: ¿Quiere _____ algo?
items.translation: (Do you want something? - formal)
items.explanation: Include 'usted' for clarity in formal. Third person verb already shows formality.

items.question: ¿Dónde vive _____?
items.translation: (Where do you live? - formal)
items.explanation: Add 'usted' to make formality explicit. Third person 'vive' indicates formal.

items.question: _____ muy amable.
items.translation: (You are very kind - formal)
items.explanation: 'Ser' third person 'es' for usted. Formal address uses él/ella forms.

items.question: ¿Cómo te _____?
items.translation: (What's your name? - informal)
items.explanation: 'Te' shows informal. Use second person 'llamas' with tú.

items.question: Por favor, _____ aquí.
items.translation: (Please, sit here - formal)
items.explanation: Formal command uses usted subjunctive + reflexive. 'Siéntese' is formal.

items.question: ¿_____ me puede ayudar?
items.translation: (Can you help me? - very formal/polite)
items.explanation: Include 'usted' at start for very polite requests.

items.question: _____ tienes razón.
items.translation: (You're right - informal)
items.explanation: Can include 'tú' for emphasis. Second person 'tienes' shows informal.

items.question: ¿Le _____ un café?
items.translation: (Can I offer you a coffee? - formal)
items.explanation: 'Le' (not 'te') indicates formal. Use with third person verbs.

items.question: _____ esperando aquí.
items.translation: (Wait here - formal command)
items.explanation: Usted command uses subjunctive form. 'Espere' is formal singular.

items.question: ¿_____ puedo preguntar algo?
items.translation: (May I ask you something? - formal)
items.explanation: 'Le' is formal indirect object. 'Te' would be informal.

items.question: _____ nombre es María.
items.translation: (Your name is María - informal)
items.explanation: 'Tu' (no accent) possessive for informal you. 'Su' for formal.

items.question: Muchas gracias, señor. _____ muy amable.
items.translation: (Thank you very much, sir. You're very kind - formal)
items.explanation: Third person 'es' for formal usted. Title 'señor' confirms formality.

items.question: ¿_____ hablas español?
items.translation: (Do you speak Spanish? - informal)
items.explanation: Subject pronoun optional with informal. Second person 'hablas' clear enough.

items.question: _____ que es una buena idea.
items.translation: (I think it's a good idea)
items.explanation: 'Creer que' is most common for 'I think'. Use with indicative.

items.question: En mi _____, es demasiado caro.
items.translation: (In my opinion, it's too expensive)
items.explanation: 'En mi opinión' is standard phrase. Formal opinion introduction.

items.question: _____ mi parecer, deberías ir.
items.translation: (In my view, you should go)
items.explanation: 'A mi parecer' means in my view. Alternative to 'en mi opinión'.

items.question: No _____ que sea correcto.
items.translation: (I don't think it's correct)
items.explanation: 'No creo que' triggers subjunctive. Negative opinion uses subjunctive.

items.question: _____ mi punto de vista, es importante.
items.translation: (From my point of view, it's important)
items.explanation: 'Desde mi punto de vista' is formal phrase. Expressing perspective.

items.question: Me _____ que tienes razón.
items.translation: (It seems to me you're right)
items.explanation: 'Parecer' (seem) for gentle opinions. 'Me parece que' is polite.

items.question: _____ de acuerdo contigo.
items.translation: (I agree with you)
items.explanation: 'Estar de acuerdo' means to agree. Fixed expression with estar.

items.question: No _____ de acuerdo.
items.translation: (I don't agree)
items.explanation: 'No estar de acuerdo' for disagreement. Polite way to disagree.

items.question: _____ mi juicio, es la mejor opción.
items.translation: (In my judgment, it's the best option)
items.explanation: 'A mi juicio' is formal opinion phrase. Similar to 'a mi parecer'.

items.question: _____ duda, vale la pena.
items.translation: (Without doubt, it's worth it)
items.explanation: 'Sin duda' means without doubt. Expresses certainty.

items.question: _____ seguro de que vendrá.
items.translation: (I'm sure he will come)
items.explanation: 'Estar seguro' for being sure/certain. Use estar with certainty.

items.question: _____ razón en eso.
items.translation: (You're right about that)
items.explanation: 'Tener razón' means to be right. Use tener, not ser/estar.

items.question: _____ la verdad, no me gusta.
items.translation: (To tell the truth, I don't like it)
items.explanation: 'A decir verdad' is fixed phrase. Honest opinion introduction.

items.question: _____ equivocado.
items.translation: (You're wrong / mistaken)
items.explanation: 'Estar equivocado' means to be wrong. Use estar for states.

items.question: _____ convencido de que sí.
items.translation: (I'm convinced that yes)
items.explanation: 'Estar convencido' for being convinced. Mental state uses estar.

items.question: ¿_____ vamos al cine?
items.translation: (Why don't we go to the movies?)
items.explanation: '¿Por qué no?' + nosotros form makes suggestion. Casual proposal.

items.question: _____ salir a cenar.
items.translation: (Let's go out to dinner)
items.explanation: 'Vamos a' + infinitive for suggestions. Most common let's structure.

items.question: ¿Qué tal si _____ una película?
items.translation: (How about we watch a movie?)
items.explanation: '¿Qué tal si?' + present indicative. Casual suggestion form.

items.question: _____ ir al parque.
items.translation: (We could go to the park)
items.explanation: 'Podríamos' (conditional) for polite suggestion. Softer than present.

items.question: Te _____ que descanses.
items.translation: (I suggest you rest)
items.explanation: 'Sugerir que' + subjunctive for suggestions. Triggers subjunctive mood.

items.question: ¿_____ te parece si vamos mañana?
items.translation: (What do you think if we go tomorrow?)
items.explanation: '¿Qué te parece si?' asks for opinion on suggestion. Common proposal.

items.question: _____ mejor estudiar ahora.
items.translation: (It would be better to study now)
items.explanation: Conditional 'sería' for polite suggestion. Less direct than 'es mejor'.

items.question: _____ probarlo primero.
items.translation: (You should try it first)
items.explanation: Conditional 'deberías' for gentle advice. Softer than 'debes'.

items.question: ¿_____ no descansamos un rato?
items.translation: (Why don't we rest a while?)
items.explanation: '¿Por qué no?' makes friendly suggestion. Rhetorical why not.

items.question: Te _____ que llames primero.
items.translation: (I recommend you call first)
items.explanation: 'Recomendar que' + subjunctive. Strong suggestion or recommendation.

items.question: _____ idea ir en tren.
items.translation: (It's a good idea to go by train)
items.explanation: 'Buena idea' + infinitive. Approving suggestion.

items.question: Yo que tú, _____ más temprano.
items.translation: (If I were you, I would leave earlier)
items.explanation: 'Yo que tú' + conditional for advice. Hypothetical suggestion.

items.question: ¿_____ comemos juntos?
items.translation: (Shall we eat together?)
items.explanation: Simple present as question makes suggestion. Rising intonation.

items.question: _____ mejor que esperemos.
items.translation: (It's better that we wait)
items.explanation: 'Es mejor que' + subjunctive. Strong suggestion about better option.

items.question: _____ pensarlo bien.
items.translation: (You should think about it carefully)
items.explanation: Conditional for gentle suggestion. 'Deberías' softer than obligation.

items.question: Hace _____ de perros.
items.translation: (The weather is awful - idiom)
items.explanation: 'Hacer un tiempo de perros' means terrible weather. Literally 'dog's weather'.

items.question: Me costó un _____ de la cara.
items.translation: (It cost me an arm and a leg)
items.explanation: 'Costar un ojo de la cara' means very expensive. Literally 'cost an eye'.

items.question: _____ en el aire.
items.translation: (I'm building castles in the air / daydreaming)
items.explanation: 'Hacer castillos en el aire' means unrealistic plans. Daydreaming idiom.

items.question: No tiene _____ de tonto.
items.translation: (He's no fool - idiom)
items.explanation: 'No tener un pelo de tonto' means not foolish at all. Negative emphasis.

items.question: Me _____ los dedos estudiando.
items.translation: (I killed myself studying - worked hard)
items.explanation: 'Quemarse los dedos' means work very hard. Literally 'burn fingers'.

items.question: Tiene _____ en la boca.
items.translation: (He was born with a silver spoon - privileged)
items.explanation: 'Tener pájaros en la cabeza' means scatter-brained. Different idiom.

items.question: _____ cuatro gatos en la fiesta.
items.translation: (There were hardly any people at the party)
items.explanation: 'Cuatro gatos' idiom means very few people. Literally 'four cats'.

items.question: Le _____ el agua al cuello.
items.translation: (He's in deep trouble - water to neck)
items.explanation: 'Llegar el agua al cuello' means desperate situation. Serious trouble.

items.question: Ese tema me _____ sin cuidado.
items.translation: (I couldn't care less about that topic)
items.explanation: 'Traer sin cuidado' means not care at all. Complete indifference.

items.question: _____ moscas con miel que con vinagre.
items.translation: (You catch more flies with honey than vinegar)
items.explanation: Proverb about being nice. Same meaning in both languages.

items.question: Habla por los _____.
items.translation: (He talks a lot - talks through elbows)
items.explanation: 'Hablar por los codos' means talk excessively. Literally 'talk through elbows'.

items.question: _____ la pata.
items.translation: (I messed up / made a mistake)
items.explanation: 'Meter la pata' means make blunder. Literally 'put in the paw'.

items.question: Eso es _____ aparte.
items.translation: (That's a different matter / another story)
items.explanation: 'Harina de otro costal' means different matter. Literally 'flour from another sack'.

items.question: No _____ con rodeos.
items.translation: (Don't beat around the bush)
items.explanation: 'No vengas con rodeos' means be direct. Literally 'don't come with detours'.

items.question: Me _____ de memoria.
items.translation: (I know it by heart)
items.explanation: 'Saber de memoria' means know by heart. Memorized knowledge.

items.question: El libro es _____.
items.translation: (The book is interesting)
items.explanation: Adjectives ending in -e don't change for gender. 'Interesante' for both.

items.question: La casa es _____.
items.translation: (The house is big)
items.explanation: Adjectives ending in -e are invariable for gender. 'Grande' for all.

items.question: El gato es _____.
